description:
The storm golem is a type-2 attack system, supplied with either a sonic gun or a set of reaper blades and a nanoblaster. Although the nanoblaster is a short range weapon, the exceptional accuracy of the storm golem and the nanominition’s capacity to follow the movement of its target, make it a really efficient weapon at medium range. The sonic gun, based on the double vibration effect, disembowels and shatters its targets with great ease.

Thanks to these qualities, the storm golems have imposed themselves as one of the standards among autonomous attack systems.

Thanks to the combination of the nanoblasters and the sonic gun, the storm golems prove to be exceptionally versatile units, capable of facing any kind of threat, ranging from light armored vehicles to infantry.

Contents:
• 4 storm golems (nano blaster/reaper blades)
• 1 storm golem (sonic gun) 
• 1 storm golem leader (nano blaster/reaper blades) 
• 3 cards
 -6 miniatures
-3 reference cards

boardgame: Magic: The Gathering Challenge Deck – Face the Hydra
description:
From the back of the "Face the Hydra" challenge deck, "This box contains sixty challenge cards and an instructional playmat.  To play against this challenge deck, each player must have a Magic: The Gathering deck."

The challenge deck allows 1 person or multiple people working together to play against a programmed deck.  The cards in this deck are not designed to be mixed with standard M:tG cards and have a distinct back. Players play their cards against this deck before running out of health.  The challenge deck does not play cards normally.  Each turn a card is drawn and its effect is applied immediately.

When playing against the Hydra deck, Hydra Head cards are placed in play.  The player(s) can attack 1 head each turn.  If enough damage is done to destroy a head, then 2 cards are drawn and if any are head cards, they are placed in play.  The game continues until either there are no heads in play or the player runs out of health.  Each turn, each Hydra head automatically damages the player.

When the wormhole opened up for the first time on the edge of the solar system, mankind was not ready. Governments scrambled to cobble together spaceships to investigate the anomaly, but it vanished as unexpectedly as it had first appeared.

Years later, the wormhole has returned and this time, we were prepared. Probe data shocked humanity when it revealed that a perfectly habitable planet lay on the other side, awaiting human colonization. A global effort was mounted and finally, the colony ship was ready for its maiden voyage. Built from whatever modular components were available, the ship left on its journey, carrying with it a crew of intrepid explorers, meager supplies and the hopes of planet Earth. Would they survive the expedition and start a new chapter in humanity's history on the mysterious planet?

Earth's Legacy is a co-operative, legacy, micro card game. Though the base game consists of very few components, the combination of player choice, random elements, and unique, persistent game modifications through an upgrade system, lead to a high level of replayability and expandability. The low complexity nature and minimal components of Earth's Legacy make it the perfect introduction to the legacy game genre that will appeal to gamers and non-gamers alike.

In Parallelo, each player starts with three pawns in each of six colors. Players take turns placing the pawns in indentations in a parallelogram-shaped layout on the wooden game board. Your goal is to create a parallelogram with four pawns of the same color — but since you have only three of each color, you need to make use of pawns placed by your opponent in order to do so. Whoever first creates three parallelograms wins.

Who's A Clever Dick: The Card Game For Real Smart Alecs!

This is a card based trivia game - one side has two questions, the other side individual letters (eg C, L, E, etc).  Players take turns drawing a card and asking one of the two questions of the player to their left.  If the player gets the question right, they keep the card, if they don't, the card goes back to the bottom of the deck.  The goal is to spell out "CLEVERDICK" before anyone else.  The only wrinkle is that there are also blank cards in the deck - no questions - and the player drawing one of these cards has to try and bluff the other player by making up a question.  If they pull it off, they keep the card and take one from the player, if not, the bluff-callin' player keeps the blank and take one from the asker.

Note: "Cleverdick" is actually mild slang in the UK, the equivalent of "smarty pants", you're not actually calling someone a "dick"!

boardgame: The Little Big Game of Bupkis
description:
The first player to reach zero, zilch, nothing - Bupkis! - wins the game. Players don't need to reach zero exactly to win. It's the only game in which zero is everything!

Bupkis is a card game played with 2 decks of cards. One deck is a regular deck of playing cards, and the other deck has from one to 3 numbers on them that range from zero (the Bupkis!) card up to 4 figures. Each number card has the four arithmetic operators (plus, minus, multiply and divide) on the bottom left with some or all of them in a darker font. The darker operators are the ones the player is allowed to use during the hand.

Each round the players are dealt 10 cards and randomly take a Number Card. The players must use the allowable operators on the Number Card to use all the Playing Cards in their hand to total the number or numbers on their card. The player who goes out receives the total of the cards left in the other player's hand as a score. The score is subtracted from the starting score of 500. The first player to reach 0 points is the winner.

Noggin Workshop IQ Squares challenges players to think outside the box to solve the riddle inside of the box/square.  It can be a solitaire game or played with others.  When playing with others, the players race to see which player can solve the riddle first, which is actually a rebus puzzle.  Play continues until a set number of cards are won by solving the rebus puzzle first.  The goal is to solve as many puzzles as possible without looking at the answer.

A card and board game based on trump taking. The cards are comprised of six suits. When the game begins all suits are of equal value. The player who lays out the highest number takes the hand and then moves their pawn one space forward on the game board. Every fifth or so space is marked "Dynasty" and a player landing on this space may select one of the trump tokens to place at the lowest possible space on the "trump track", in the center of the board. As the suits are added to the track, the order in which suits may trump each other follows. For instance, the first placed (and therefore lowest on the track) suit is jesters. Jesters can trump all cards. The next placed suit (set above jesters on the track) is kings.  Jesters will still trump all cards but kings, kings trump all cards. If a player lands on a "dynasty" spot and all six suits have been set, they may swap two suits. Game ends when one player has traversed the entire board.

In Carrrds!, players compete to gain ownership of the most shreds of a torn up treasure map that leads to the pirate treasure. Each turn, you may ask another player for a piece of a certain map. Each map piece can be identified by a certain symbol such as a hook or a key. Carrrds! becomes extra fun when you throw in the action cards: swords, guns, parrots, voodoo dolls and flags can all be played to introduce an element of chaos and unpredictability into play. There are even unique pirate cards such as the rogue, the gunslinger, and the voodoo lady.

Carrrds! is flexible. You don't have to use all the cards. The easiest way to start playing is to just use the island cards, swords and guns. Voodoo dolls, flags, parrots and pirate cards offer some additional gameplay options for intrepid pirates.

Expansion: extra action cards
Included in the deck are some extra action cards: the pirate flag, the voodoo doll and the parrot. These special cards allow you to interrupt the normal game flow even further. But watch out, the game could get pretty complicated!

Expansion: pirate cards
Also included in the deck are six unique pirate cards. Each player gets one pirate card that only they can see at the start of play and places it face down in front of them. You can then use your pirate card's ability at any point during the game to surprise another player.

Bolster your armies with Heroes & Legends, the third expansion for Warmachine: High Command!

Heroes & Legends contains 5 copies each of 16 different cards, plus 4 additional warcaster cards, to bring new strategies to your Cygnar, Khador, Protectorate of Menoth, and Cryx decks.

Requires Warmachine: High Command core set to play.

Rightgames LLC and Snowbird studio have released Eador: Masters of the Broken World board game based on "Eador" strategy computer games series. Action takes place in a fantasy world of Eador which is broken into pieces of a past holistic world, surfing across the Astral. Players take roles of the Masters, mighty demigods, trying to subordinate as many Eador shards as possible. They forward armies to conquer new territories, cast spells and perform powerful rituals.

The board game suits for 2-6 players but it’s mostly comfortable to play for at least 4 players. All players get their Master cards at the game start – such cards exist not only for the atmosphere, they also help to settle some controversies. 12-18 shard cards (amount depends on the number of players) are mixed up to a separate pile and the game is over when this pile flows out. There are always two shard cards on the table, the one for the current round and another for the next – it helps players to plan their moves concentrating on the shards which are easier to get. Shard conquering is performed by playing action cards; there are more then 100 of them – armies, spells and rituals. At the game beginning everyone gets 7 action cards to the hand. Usually it’s possible to add only one more card during the turn so using them should be careful. Rituals are the cards with especially strong features letting, for example, to take more action cards, take someone else’s action or shard cards, to gain more winning points. Player can’t fight for the chard during the turn if he performs a ritual but these cards give him further advantage.

At the beginning of the round each player decides if he will fight for the current shard or not. You need to play an army or a spell card to join a battle. The most important feature of the army is its power. Also there are the type of the army on the card (Light, Chaos or neutral), its class (warrior, monster, magician or undead) and nature of the ground (hills, swamps, plains, forests). Armies and some spells stay in the battle to the end of the round, some spells work only for the round they were played at. The result of the battle round is counted when all players interested in the shard have made their moves. The owner of the strongest army gets authority points counters according to his advantage above the second contender. Players can give up from the second turn and leave the battle. In this case they can’t play armies or spells anymore for the current battle but still have a chance to win if nobody acquires more authority points. When all players pass the battle ends and the shard goes to the player who gained maximum authority points.

Each battle has some pieces of subtleties. Firstly, it’s impossible to play Light and Chaos armies without a special card. Secondly, all the shards are different: some of them give advantages to defined type or class of armies or restrict using them, others allow to play two armies in a round. Thirdly, all armies fighting for the shard at the “native” land get extra strength. Attentive player will get a big profit considering all these features. When battle for the last shard is over players count their winning points on their conquered shards and performed rituals. That sum shows the winner.

The game for two or three players differs – they get 3 or 2 Master cards and make more actions during the round – to play a ritual and take part in the battle.

An educational board game themed around recycling and conservation, designed in Australia.

The sole mechanic is roll and move without the rolling, as each player moves exactly three spaces on each turn.  Recyclable material tokens are distributed evenly between the two to four players. They move along the branches on the board. When they land on an appropriate recycling spot in their turn, they can leave a token.  The first player to recycle everything wins.

The game also comes with a poster and a "Personal Pollution Meter" (printed on the box insert).

Each player gets 3 meeples, which he has to move from the starting space to the center of the game board. The moves are determined by a die. If a meeple lands on a space of another color, it has to go back to the last free space of its own color. The same happens when a meeple is thrown out by another meeple. The first player who gets all 3 meeples on the the center spaces wins the game.

Each player gets their own deck, and builds a down pile. Then they deal out four cards face-up next to it. As soon as everyone is ready, someone shouts "GO!" and all the players simultaneously begin starting number piles in the center of the table, and playing on each others number piles.

The number piles are not removed from the table for the duration of the round, and continue to grow and change very rapidly throughout the game. Players can play a card higher, or lower than whatever number is showing on the top of any given pile, but they do need to be sure to match the color of the pile. Also, players can place a wild card on any pile and announce a number, and a color, and then play a card higher or lower than the number they called, in the new color on that pile.

The speed and excitement build until someone who has played all of their down pile shouts "Flip Flash!" and everyone must stop. Next, the players gather and sort all of the cards that have been played in the center of the table and sort them, so that each player can count up how many cards they played. The score is figured by taking the number of cards that were played, and subtracting double the number of cards that were still in each players down pile.

WHAT MAKES FLIP FLASH DIFFERENT:
The ability to play up or down on the number piles, and the wild card twist, speed up the game by making sure that you rarely stay stuck for long! The number piles are never capped off, or removed from play during a round, and players flip through their hand-deck one card at a time. The three suits keeps things interesting, but limits confusion as well.

Flip Flash is different than Nay-Jay! in the number and design of the cards, and a rule change. Flip Flash includes 4 decks of 60 cards each.

Heroes, Villains and Drawing Awesome Levels!

1-4 Players, 10-30min, Free Print and Play Game (one sheet double sided)


A dice-rolling, create-your-own-adventure game to rekindle those drawing and storytelling skills from childhood.

Choose a Villain, build some challenges, then take your Hero through them.

Spend your rolls on abilities, then roll a sum total to beat the challenge.

You will be trying to roll a sum total with 3 dice, then compare that to the 3 target numbers shown on the challenge.

Each challenge you attempt, will allow you to accumulate points.

However, you can only BANK those points when you choose to WALK AWAY and not attempt another challenge.

If you run out of health and are Eliminated while attempting a challenge, you will lose all the points you've accumulated so far at that difficulty level (on that Villain Card).

The player who survives with the Most Points BANKED by the end of 4 difficulty levels/rounds wins.


Download the PnP PDF below in the "FILES" section of this game page.
Hope you enjoy!!

Created in October 2011, Tails and Tactics had been in development for almost two years before being released in July 2013. Creator Pedro “Reynard” Loustaunau first started with a very serious game concept and quickly found it to be lame. After realizing it needed a sillier tone, he quickly went from “a little bit lighthearted” to “quite a bit silly” as he realized Jan’s art would be perfect for his new vision of the game. Having found his ad in the programming schedule for Anthrocon 2011, Reynard contacted Jan with his crazy idea for him to become Lead Artist for the project. The ad pointed to Swords and Sausages (now Reynard’s favorite webcomic), which showcased Jan’s vibrant imagination and his talents as an artist.

Jan quickly became the Lead Artist for Tails and Tactics and development continued for almost two years before finally releasing at Anthrocon 2013. Thanks to the pledges of many wonderful people on IndieGoGo, Series 1 of Tails and Tactics became a reality.

Objective of the Game:
To win. Duh.
In order to do that, though, you must fight through your opponent's front line and attack his or her base enough to deplete its health to 0 - before they do so to you.
Each player begins the game with 30 Base Health. Base Health is lost when a unit controlled by an opposing player attacks your base (represented by you, the player). They may only do so, though, when you have no units on the field to defend with. If you do have units on the field that are able to defend, opponents must remove them before being able to attack your base directly.
1 base health is lost per attack, no matter how powerful (or weak) the attacking unit is. Some powerful units make up for this by having the ability to attack multiple times, so watch out!
Base health may also be lost when an opponent attacks one of your units and you choose to dodge the attack. Any dodged attacks will be applied directly to your base.
Once your opponent's base health reaches 0, you are victorious! Gloating and victory dances are acceptable at this point.

boardgame: 8-Bit Swordsman: Clash For the Prism of Punishment
description:
The Prism of Punishment awards its bearer with unfathomable strength and power, but it must be earned through a trial of strength and the bloodshed of your opponents (some Human, others not so much). The combatants take turns playing cards for movement around the arena, attacking their opponent, and scoring points. The first player to earn 15 points (and not have Curse: The Second Journey successfully played on them in response) has earned enough Shards to fuse them together to complete the Prism and earn victory!

Features and Components:


    4 player tokens
    10 rocks which hinder moves/attacks, placed by the players on the field prior to beginning the game.
    72 Cards, 2 Types(Equipment cards for moves/attacks, or Curses: cards which hinder opponents while poking fun at/giving a nod to old-school rpgs.)
    2 double-sided game mats, printed and designed to form a storage box!

Glyph is a card game system designed to be able to play an enormous variety of games and take up a small amount of space. The deck of 98 cards can be whittled down to a standard 52-card deck to play all the classics, though it contains 6 full standard suits and plenty more, including story suits for storytelling games (with images), 3 colored suits, and 6 minor elemental suits that have no problem sharing their cards with other elemental suits.

The cards are orientation-dependent, so the properties they take on will differ depending on whether they are played right-side-up or upside-down.

In addition to playing some of your favorite card games, you can use the deck to create new games. The different types of suits can be played separately or mixed and matched for tons of unique gameplay possibilities.

Some of the ways the cards can be organized are:

- Six suits (HEARTS, CROSSES, CLOVERS, TOADSTOOLS, DIAMONDS, and HORNS) numbered 0-14. Aces, Jacks, Queens, and Kings are further marked for use in a standard deck.

- Three suits (RED, BLUE, and GREEN) numbered 0-29.

- Two full sets of six suits (EARTH, FIRE, WATER, AIR, MAGIC, and ALCHEMY), numbered 2-9 plus the additional classes ACE, APPRENTICE, MAGICIAN, and WIZARD. These suits come 1, 2, or 3 to a card.

- Five story suits (WILD, ITEM, EVENT, CHARACTER, and LOCATION). Each card in the deck has a different image that can be used in creative and storytelling games.

The deck also contains 8 wild cards that can be organized by color (making 4 groups of 2) or by layout (making 2 groups of 4).

boardgame: Star Wars: The Card Game – It Binds All Things
description:
"The Force is what gives a Jedi his power. It’s an energy field created by all living things. It surrounds us and penetrates us; it binds the galaxy together."
   –Obi-Wan Kenobi, Star Wars: A New Hope

It Binds All Things is the fifth and penultimate Force Pack in the "Echoes of the Force" cycle for Star Wars: The Card Game, and it continues the cycle's progression deeper into the mysteries of the Force with ten new objective sets (two copies each of five different sets) that allow you to follow a new Jedi version of Leia Organa to the forest moon of Endor, experience the boundless loyalty of a Wookiee, or use fate cards in entirely new ways with the Empire's Imperial Navy.

Even as the different affiliations of Star Wars: The Card Game explore the possibilities offered by the "Echoes of the Force" cycle, you may discover that the best way to gain control of the Force struggle is a targeted offense that directly attacks your opponent's Force-committed units. To that end, It Binds All Things also introduces one of the galaxy's most feared Bounty Hunters, the Assassin Droid IG-88.

The Force holds the galaxy of Star Wars together and developing your devotion to the Force may determine the outcome of your future games!

Contains objective sets 117-121.

boardgame: Mystery of the Abbey with The Pilgrims' Chronicles
description:
Mystery of the Abbey is a whodunit game with a twist. A monk has been murdered in a medieval French Abbey. Players maneuver their way through the Abbey examining clues and questioning each other to find out who is the culprit. A masterful game of deduction!

The Pilgrims' Chronicles is a 12 card-expansion for Mystery of the Abbey published for the Essen 2005 Fair.

The 2007 Days of Wonder reprint of Mystery of the Abbey included the Pilgrims' Chronicles expansion.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: The Horus Heresy: Book One – Betrayal
description:
The Horus Heresy Book One: Betrayal is a supplement for Warhammer 40,000 dealing with war and battle in the dark age of the Horus Heresy. This book, lavishly illustrated in full colour, contains extensive background information on the Imperium as it stood at the end of the Great Crusade. Also detailed are the histories of the four Legions who took part: the Sons of Horus, Emperor's Children, World Eaters and Death Guard and an extensive campaign system.

This book also contains a complete Space Marine Legion Crusade Army list as well as games rules for the Primarchs of the four Legions, super-heavy vehicles and special characters featured in the story.

This is a leather bound full colour 290 page hardback book, leatherbound with embossed and silvered text.

boardgame: The Horus Heresy: Book Two – Massacre
description:
Word of Horus’ treachery escapes Isstvan III and now the Legions loyal to the Emperor mobilise to make Horus and those who follow account for their crimes. However the depth of the betrayal is yet to be revealed.

The Horus Heresy Book Two: Massacre is lavishly illustrated in full colour and contains the dark deeds leading up to the terrible slaughter that unfolded during the Isstvan V Dropsite Massacre. Also detailed are the histories of the four legions that took part: The Iron Hands, Salamanders, Night Lords and Word Bearers, and an extensive campaign system so you can play out the battles on the tabletop. This book also contains additional entries for the Space Marine Legion Crusade Army list presented in The Horus Heresy Book One: Betrayal, as well as games rules for the Primarchs of the four newly described Legions, super-heavy vehicles and special characters featured in the story.

The book itself is a full colour 286 page hardback, leather bound with embossed and silvered text.

The playing pieces for this games are of different shapes, each made of five cubes glued together. There are six different shapes, each in one color. Additionally, the game contains a color die, two building bases and some scoring chips.

The bases are placed on the table and a handful of pieces are stacked onto them. However, the bases aren't straight, so the pieces have to interlock to avoid falling. Players take turns to roll a die, take a piece in the corresponding color and adding it to either of the two structures. For each row of cubes that is added, the player scores a point. An additional point is scored for adding to the higher of the two towers. Points are deducted when a tower collapses.

The game ends when the last piece has been added to the tower. The player with most points is the winner.

This is a fun fast paced card game for 2 to 6 players.  The goal of the game is to be the first player to have five point cards in your hand equaling zero.  How do you do that?  There are several strategies you can use.  But, they all stem from taking the cards you don't want out of your hand and making them your opponent’s problem.

At Its Most Basic

There are 88 cards in each deck.  There are three groups of cards.  Each group of cards has their own purpose.  Some hinder, some hurt, and some will win you the game.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Hungry Higgs is played with two dice with sides 1, 2 and 3, and pieces on a balancing board. Every one of the play pieces has got a certain weight that can have decisive influence on the game.

The main strategy is to balance between caution (don’t be knocked out by your opponents game piece) and greed (capture as much prey as possible).

Figure it out:

Hungry Higgs is played on a sort of tilt. This means that weight close to the middle of the board will appear to weigh less than weight situated longer away from the middle. If you capture prey on your opponents half of the board it will appear heavier than if you had captured it from your own half of the board.

It is also a good idea to figure out the probability for being knocked home by your opponent.

Steal space:

If you can place your game piece in the middle of the board – but in safe distance from your opponent – do it. Thus your opponent will have trouble to make a safe move when it is his turn.

Find out what kind of risk you want to take to find the balance between capturing prey, stealing space or being knocked home.

Tricks:

Keeping up appearances:

Perhaps your opponent is able to knock you home – but he might be more interested in capturing preys. Don’t let him discover that he can knock you home.

Published with three games in one box:

Decisive Battle of the Combined Fleet

"Decisive Battle the Combined Fleet" is a tactical level surface naval battle wargame in WW2 Pacific theater. Players control either the fleet of Imperial Japanese Navy or United States Navy, and fight each other. Players manage ships movement, gun and torpedo attack. This game was designed for wargame beginners.

F-16 Fighting Falcon

"F-16 Fighting Falcon" is a tactical level air combat wargame in modern warfare. This game covers only air to air combat, and does not include ground attack. Victory conditions vary by scenario. For example, shooting down enemy aircraft. This game was designed for wargame beginners.

Clash in the South Pacific

"Crash at Southern Pacific" is an operational level wargame for Battle of the Santa Cruz Island. Players control either the aircraft carrier fleet of Imperial Japanese Navy or United States Navy. Players manage fleet movement, search for the enemy, and aircraft operation. This game was designed for wargame beginners.

Mix between Mathable and Dominoes. Domino tiles may be placed next to the domino placed in the middle of the table to complete any equation (+, -, x, ÷) so that one of the two numbers on the domino placed is the result of the equation formed by the two adjacent numbers, regardless if the two numbers of the equation are from the same domino or two different dominoes.

The player who gets rid of their dominoes first is the winner.

Like in Mathable players place the number tiles on the Scrabble-like board. Tile what is placed must be result of addition of subtraction of 2 adjacent tiles next to it horizontally or vertically. Some squares also have special symbols. Players have less tiles in hand than original Mathable. Each tile placed counts its value as points.

Game ends if any player plays his last tile or everybody passes.

Player with the most points is the winner.

boardgame: Munchkin Sparkly Good Fairy Shot Glass
description:
Munchkin is not a drinking game. Really.

But don't let that stop you from showing your love of Munchkin with the Munchkin Sparkly Good Fairy Shot Glass. These 1.5 oz glasses are white with the Sparkly Good Fairy in pink, and have LEDs in the base that flash when a switch in the base of the glass is pressed. The lights represent the Fairy's magic, enhancing your own feeble efforts!

Munchkin Sparkly Good Fairy Shot Glass Rule

The Munchkin Sparkly Good Fairy Shot Glass may be used in any Munchkin game or its sequels.
When fighting a monster, either as the main fighter or as a helper, you may push the button on the Munchkin Sparkly Good Fairy Shot Glass to turn the pretty lights on and then drink one complete shot* to double the combat bonus of a single Potion. You may do this as many times as you like during a game, but only once per monster, and you must discard two cards for every Potion after the first per game. You must turn the lights back off after each use, and if you forget to turn them on before the shot is poured, the effect is wasted (but you still have to drink the shot).

     The owner of the Shot Glass is the sole judge of what liquid(s) may be used.

The fifth expansion set for the Fantasy Flight Games version of Cosmic Encounter, Cosmic Dominion has the unique distinction of having been designed and developed by the fan base of Cosmic Encounter, comprising nearly two years of collaboration on both Facebook and BGG.

Cosmic Dominion adds 30 new aliens to the FFG canon. These will include a few favorite classics from the good old days, with a large number of new alien powers contributed by a wide variety of fan designers.

The set also includes a brand-new Reward Deck that can stand alone or be mixed with the one in Cosmic Incursion, but that is composed almost entirely of new cards, many with interesting twists on standard ones.

Finally, included are special "Special Ship Markers" that can be used for a variety of variants, with some included in the rulebook to start you off.

Provided to players who participate in the Zombicide Gaming Night #2 Organized Play events

Capture your opponent's last queen to win.

4 variants (4 2-D perspectives of a spherical, 3-D game):

white whole | black quartered (shown below)
black whole | white quartered
white halved N & S | black halved E & W
black halved N & S | white halved E & W
A draw is impossible.
Each player begins the game with 8 queens.
Once the last queen of either player has been captured, the game will end immediately.

The pendulum-move rule applies which dictates double-moves alternating between the two players throughout the game except for the very first move which is only a single-move by white.

All available 4-, 5-, 6-, 7- directional moving pieces can be half-promoted into pieces with 1 additional direction of movement at the cost of 3 moves.

All available 4- & 6- directional moving pieces can be single-promoted into pieces with 2 additional directions of movement at the cost of 4 moves.

All rooks (4-directional moving pieces) can be double-promoted into queens with 4 additional directions of movement at the cost of 6 moves.

All pieces capable of movement can capture by replacement any enemy or neutral pieces.

Portals connect opposite edges along all 8 geometrically-contiguous directions of linear movement possible within the square-spaced, flat 2-D gameboard.
Consequently, 8 directions of movement are available from every square-space within the flat 2-D gameboard.
Essentially, this is a spherical surface, 3-D chess variant that can be represented accurately and played well using as few or as many of the 4 available, congruent opening setups as desirable within the flat 2-D gameboard due to its exclusive usage of 2-D pieces.
This means that an entire game played via any 1 given opening setup can be completed transposed, move-by-move, into entire games played via all of the other congruent opening setups.
Notably, the naive appearance of geometrical asymmetry in 2-D between the two armies with any given opening setup is totally illusionary.
When the fact that the 2-D gameboard has no real edges in any way (i.e., continuous space), despite its flat 2-D representation to the contrary, is taken into account, the positions and available moves for all of the 2-D pieces of the white army, relative to everything (friendly pieces, neutral pieces, enemy pieces and empty spaces), are mirror-image symmetrical to those of the black army.
In reality, a 4-fold, perfect, holistic 3-D geometrical symmetry applicable to the surface of a sphere exists for this game despite the limitations due to its flat 2-D representations.

This is the disadvantage to using flat 2-D representations of a spherical-surface, 3-D chess variant.

The 2 most symmetrical, representative, opening setups (white whole - black quartered & black whole - white quartered) out of 4 available maintain perfect quadrilateral symmetry in 2-D by north-south, east-west, northwest-southeast and northeast-southwest axes.
The playing surface consisting of a finite, 2-D plane with a square-spaced, flat 2-D gameboard of 20 x 20 spaces (400 spaces total) and a square shape overall (apparently) is perfectly mapped onto the surface of a finite 3-D sphere.

[Note: The true shape overall of the square-spaced, flat 2-D gameboard is actually circular. This is not obvious. Instead, it appears to be square due to the commonplace elongation of the diagonal dimension and/or shortening of the orthogonal dimension which is the universal convention for representing them.
This implicates why a perfect mapping onto the surface of a 3-D sphere, from a circle rather than a square, is ideally suitable and achievable.]

Fortunately, the players are not required to deal with any complex, confusing 3-D curvature characteristic to a spherical playing surface.

This is the advantage to using flat 2-D representations of a spherical-surface, 3-D chess variant.

Black Friday is about danger and power. Expect to use the Ultrared Mode (see Toxic City Mall) every time, slaughter Zombies with the best weapons around, and above all, reach unprecedented body counts. description:
To Set Up:
1. Insert the holes inside the front feet into the tabs on the side of the Bone Bucket. 
2. Carefully place the tab bars inside both front feet. 
3. Lock Bruno into a sleeping position by pushing Bruno's head down and turning it slightly counterclockwise until it locks into place. 
4. Place the bones into the Bone Bucket, mix them well, and slide the Power Switch to On. 
5. Shuffle the Bone Cards and place them face down in front of Bruno.

To Play:
1. After deciding the order of play, the first player takes one card from the top of the pile and shows it to the other players. The Player then removes the number and color of bones from the Bone Bucket as indicated on the card.

2. After the player successfully removes the bones indicated on the card, the next player draws a card from the pile and so on.

3. Following the instructions on the card, the players take turns carefully removing the bones from the Bone Bucket.

4. Whenever a player disturbs the Bone Bucket and wakes Bruno, he will jump and bark at the player, disqualifying them from the game. After putting Bruno back to sleep (see Step 3 in the To Set Up section), the other players continue to remove bones in turn until only one player remains that player is the winner.

5. If players remove all the bones from the Bone Bucket without walking Bruno, the game ends in a tie.

6. If a player draws a blank card, that player may skip their turn without having to remove any bones. You can also use blank cards as replacements for cards that are lost or damaged.

Contents:
Barking Bruno Body
Bone Bucket Case
25 Cards
- 5 Blank Cards
- 4 Sets of 5 Cards (Each set contains 3 Single Bones, 1 Double Bone, 1 Triple Bone)
32 Bones
- 8 Red bones
- 8 Blue bones
- 8 White bones
- 8 Yellow bones

Requires three AA size batteries

Nice Mice, Twice Mice, Mice on Ice! It's Yahtzee, meets Uno, meets Parcheesi!

Mice & Dice is a family-friendly game that is fun for all ages! Choose or roll your number (d6) in the beginning of the game. If YOU roll your number on your turn, you get 2 points. If anyone else rolls your number on THEIR turn, you get 1 point. Every 5 points that you obtain you get to roll an extra dice (starting with one, max of 5). If anyone, including you, ever rolls your number two times or more you get to choose a mice or rat card. Mice cards help you, Rat cards hurt other players (i.e. Fat Rat- Roll 1 less die). After scoring 25 points, you enter the cheese phase where you then have the option of pulling cheese cards if more than one of your number is rolled. Cheese cards give you extra points based on the cheese card. The first player to 50 points wins!

The game is for 2-6 players and takes about 15 minutes to play.

boardgame: Russian Empire 1912 (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description:
A fan expansion of the popular board game Ticket to Ride designed for 2-5 players. Contains a large map of the Russian Empire back to 1912, 54 destination tickets (48 regular routes and 6 long routes).

To play the components from the original game are required, be it Europe, USA or any other genuine edition with train cars and stations as well as illustrated cards of wagons and locomotives.

The basic rules of the Europe edition are applied to the game setup and main principles concerning Tunnels and Ferries. Double routes are used only with 4-5 players, otherwise only one track is accessible. Please note that some drastic changes were introduced into the scoring system. To evaluate your progress and receive earned winning points it is recommended to use a new scoring table, presented at the right bottom corner of the board allowing to assess built tunnels and regular routes separately.

At the end of the game some bonuses +10 might be awarded for players' achievements such as the longest route, the biggest number of visited cities or any other trophy at your option.


NOTE: "Ticket to Ride" and "Days of Wonder" are registered trademarks of Days of Wonder, Inc. This fan-created expansion map is not a Days of Wonder product, but has been created with permission of Days of Wonder, Inc, solely for unpaid, non-commercial use. Some of the text and illustrations used herein may be Copyright © 2004-2014, Days of Wonder, Inc. All Rights Reserved.


В основу данной версии игры легла гипсометрическая карта Российской империи лохматого 1912 года, содержащая не только опыт изображения рельефа нашей необъятной Родины, но и основные ветки железнодорожных перегонов того времени. Разумеется, существовавших в начале 20 века путей оказалось недостаточно для создания более менее сбалансированной игровой карты, а потому были добавлены несуществовавшие, а частично даже сейчас несуществующие маршруты, тоннели и паромные переправы (то есть на особую историчность и реалистичность не претендую). За неимением сколько-нибудь крупных городов в северо-восточной части Российской империи в качестве игровых узлов были взяты либо губернские центры, либо военные посты, основанные еще при освоении Сибири. Часть этих поселений в наши дни практически неизвестна и представляет подчас еще более жалкое зрелище, чем тогда, в царское время - счет населения в некоторых из них ведется не то что на тысячи, а лишь сотни человек.Постарался выдержать оригинальный стиль игры. Поле довольно большое, рассчитано на 2-5 человек. Для двоих даже великовато, но это, как с Европой и Америкой. Маршруты довольно разветвленные, но имеются и узкие места. Для игры рекомендуется использовать базовые правила Европы - двойные маршруты только для 3+ человек, классический принцип строительства туннелей и переправ, то же число вытягиваемых и оставляемых коротких/длинных маршрутов. При желании можно и похоумрулить. Внесены, на мой взгляд, логичные изменения в механику подсчета очков - туннельные ветки стали несколько ценнее, что нашло свое отражение в таблице подсчета очков, приведенной в правом нижнем углу поля. В конце игры возможно добавление участникам стандартных бонусов +10 очков за наиболее длинный маршрут или максимальное количество посещенных городов.


ВНИМАНИЕ: "Ticket to Ride" и "Days of Wonder" являются зарегистрированными торговыми марками компании Days of Wonder, Inc. Это любительское дополнение не является продуктом Days of Wonder, но создано с разрешения Days of Wonder, Inc исключительно для безвозмездного, некоммерческого использования. Часть текста и иллюстраций, использованных в дополнении, могут являтся объектом авторского права Copyright © 2004-2014, Days of Wonder, Inc. Все права защищены.

description:
The Trans-Clyde Game was designed by David G. Watts (Railway Rivals and others) for the Greater Glasgow Passenger Transport Executive as part of publicity to show how easy it was to get around Glasgow and the surrounding areas (of west Central Scotland) using public transport.

It is a race game comprising a number of short rounds (the rules suggest 3 or 4), where each player starts from a randomly allocated railway station, and must visit three places of interest (in any order) using a combination of overground rail, underground/subway system (affectionately known as "The Clockwork Orange") or buses, then return to their start point to win that round. The person to get back "home" first scores 5 points, the others scoring one point for each site of interest reached. The overall winner is the person with most points at the end of the pre-agreed number of rounds.

The board is made up of a hexagonal base, overprinted with the main rail routes, stations and places of interest (that the players must visit). Each round has a different theme - shopping centres, scenic locations, sports stadia, etc. Players roll two dice if they want to travel by train or subway, and one die if travelling by bus. Certain die rolls will result in "incident" cards, which may force a detour, a missed turn etc.

2-6 players, but best with 3-4.

Components:

Game Board
Rules (1p)
2 6-sided dice
8 Hexagonal plastic playing pieces with a Bus on one face, and a Train on the opposite
30 Destination Cards
14 Rail Incident Cards
14 Bus Incident Cards

In MindMaze: Verzwickte Rätsel - Wahre Geschichten, players take turns asking questions to try to solve criminal mysteries.

In general, a referee tells a strange story, then the players must determine why and how the story happened by asking "Yes" or "No" questions, with "Not Important" also being a plausible answer. Players take turns asking questions, with a point being subtracted for each question. Players are awarded points for asking certain key questions that eliminate faux versions of the story. The list of useful key questions is attached to every riddle. This sort of gaming procedure effectively encourages players to develop careful analytical questions and eliminates random questions.

"Die Rheinreise. Gesellschafts-Spiel für die Deutsche Jugend. Neue verbesserte Ausgabe" - The journey along the Rhine. Board game for the German youth. New and improved edition.

Object: be the first to reach target space crossing 100 numbered squares. Typical 'roll and move' game.
special: along your way to the target you'll pick up tools (umbrella, boat...) which make your race a bit easier/faster

Contents: 4-piece, color illustrated and designed with some black and white images cardboard and the center of the Rhine River in the stretch between Mainz and Cologne (34x69 cm); pawns of tin in an old painting (6 persons, 4 ships); 26 colorfully illustrated cards, 1 dice.

MicroSport Shuffle is a game of dexterity and skill based on popular British pub games dating back to the 15th Century.

On his turn, a player flicks four coins across a board marked with horizontal bands called beds. If a coin lands entirely within a bed (that is, without touching a horizontal line), he scores a point. Points are scored after each flick, so earlier coins may score multiple times, as long as they bumped into another bed. After his turn, the four coins are removed from the board and the game proceeds with the next player.

The object of the game is to score three points in each of the five beds. If a player lands a coin in a completed bed (where he already has three points), a point is awarded to his opponent instead. The winner is the first player to score fifteen points.

A simple game published about the German City of Essen. 
By playing the players get to know a little bit about the city and its shops.
During each player's turn they roll a six-sided dice to move along a game-board depicting a map of Essen. At certain spaces players can acquire cards of the shops or they can go sightseeing by following the instructions on the back of the card. Sometimes there is only information written, sometimes you have to pay an amount of money or at other times you may earn some cash.

The game ends when the last player reaches the finish space with his playing piece. The winner is the person who has acquired the most sightseeing and/or shop cards.

Once upon a time, in 2 neighboring villages at war, 1 of the peasants was not like the others...He was sent to create havoc and take out as many of the enemy inhabitants as he could unnoticed.

One day, both villages caught on to the other's sinister plot, and the manhunt to expose the traitor began! And neither village will be at peace until either the enemy's residents, or the other's rogue, is destroyed!


    Includes 2 8-card decks (5 Peasants, 2 Guards, 1 Rogue per player.).


The objective of this game is to expose the other player's Rogue card, or kill all of their Peasants, before they can do the same to you. Each turn, the players draw 3 cards and each choose 1 from the other player's hand before discarding and drawing another 3 cards. Should a Peasant be revealed, nothing happens. Should a Guard show his face, your opponent gets to reveal an additional card from your hand for that turn. Should the Rogue be revealed, you win. This was designed as a very quick, as well as easy to learn, mini-game.

One final mechanic worth mentioning: should a player have their Rogue card in hand and it is not revealed that turn, both decks and discard piles are reshuffled and their opponent removes one of their Peasants from their deck.

boardgame: Es war einmal: Magische Märchen & Ritterliche Romanzen
description: Es war einmal: Magische Märchen & Ritterliche Romanzen seems to consist of Once Upon a Time: Knightly Tales and Once Upon a Time: Enchanting Tales, which can be added to the base game separately or in combination to add new storytelling possibilities.

From the Kickstarter page:

"Every three years, in the prosperous city of Matilda, an event is held in which the lord of the city will choose the finest and bravest warriors to join the ranks of the Matilda Defenders. This battle tournament tests the team's tactics and strategies to convince the lord and to show the citizens which team is worthy to be part of the renowned Matilda Defenders."

Inspired by a number of tactical games played over the course of our gaming years, including: Tactics Ogre, Front Mission 3, Rhapsody, Disgaea, Vandal Hearts, Vanguard Bandits, and lastly, the game that inspired us the most, Final Fantasy Tactics.

Kill all enemies - the word says it all, simply wipe out all enemies on the battlefield. Protect your leader and defeat the opposing side leader.

As the leader of your Viking clan, your goal is to earn the most honor in all of Dragonia. But beware, wild dragons and rival clans will engage you in battle, stealing your honor and preventing you from collecting treasures.

Vikings of Dragonia is a 2-5 player game of strategy, risk, and reward. Your goal is to become the next ruler of Dragonia by being the first to have the most honor of all. On a modular board and deck of power cards, players choose from a wide selection of options on their turn to expand their territory, increase their honor, or engage in battles with rival clans.

Will you be the next ruler of Dragonia?

description:
At the beginning of the game, each player secretly receives a team card. They don't know who will be their partner, so they'll have to judge everyone fairly.
At the beginning of the round, each player gets 7 cards with a piece of lyrics on them. They proceed to draft all the cards until they receive one last from their neighbor. Then, they all try to compose a short song with 5 of their cards. They can choose to use the lyrics, or to flip the card over to the "Tralalaa" card instead. When everyone is ready, players take turns singing the song they just wrote (humming, shouting or beatboxing on each card on the "Tralalaa" face), with the tune and musical genre of their choice. After each performance, other players each award one point or zero to the player who just sang. But don't be to harsh: remember, anyone can be on your team!

Be the first reaching the target (Stuttgart, space 110); learn more about the black forest. Simple 'Roll & Move' game - moving by turns.

boardgame: ...And One for All! Supplement 1: Additional Rules Variants & Options
description: This two-page PDF is a menu of rules which players may selectively opt to include in game scenarios. Some rules change core game mechanisms while others specify behaviors for specific pieces in the Eureka Miniatures 40 mm Musketeer product line.

Dominaedro is a abstract game played with 9 d12 dice, pieces of dominoes and some counters.

Players roll the d12 dice to create a 3x3 grid. Then they pick 3 dominoes pieces, and on their turn they put one piece between the dice, following placement rules. Players try to make the best combinations to get the most points.

At the end of the game, the player with the most points is the winner.

From the publisher´s website:
Roll the multi-colored dice to create the combinations on the tiles. The one who gets the most combinations, wins the game! Color Yatzy makes it possible for younger children to also enjoy the thrill of yatzy, and the die cup make rolling simple even for the littlest of hands! High quality dice and the simple tiles make this game easy for everyone to learn, and the small size also makes it ideal for travel!

The goal of the game is to move one's car from the starting space to the gas station and from there to the restaurant and back again. The first player to complete this tour wins the game. On their way, the players have to mind the traffic rules. At the traffic lights, a color die determines if it's stop or go!

Each player gets a car and starts from a starting space (A-F).
The Goal is to move through both roundabouts back to ones starting place and mind the traffic rules. The first player to achieve this wins the game. The colored spaces symbolize traffic lights: yellow = stop for 1 turn, red = stop for 2 turns, green = get one additional die roll.

Light and fun, Stratagem is an abstract strategy board game strikes a perfect balance of luck and strategy that casual gamers will really enjoy.

This turn-based game can be played with 2 to 6 players.  Players take turns rolling a die for movement and claiming spaces on a hexagonal board.  When claimed spaced are converted to controlled spaces players score points.  The first player to reach a target score wins.

Action cards can be played to support or hinder any player's progress.  On each player's turns all players have an opportunity to play one card if they want to.

Not to be confused with:

        Stratagem (1998)
        StrataGem (2004)

Pioneers is a thematic card game inspired by the old video game Oregon Trail. You play as a pioneer crossing the United States in the 1830's. You gather supplies, play action cards, and encounter events that can help you along, or hinder your travels. The goal is to get to the west coast first by spending resources to travel to cities. You will be collecting supply cards, surviving events, and interacting with other players.

Gameplay: Every player starts the game with a party leader card that grants special abilities along with 3 party members. If all 3 party members die, you lose and the game continues. In the center of the table are destination decks that every player is trying to travel to. To travel to a destination, you spend the required resources shown, acquire the card, and move on to the next destination. The player that gets the set of 2 rivers, and 3 cities, ends the game and wins after a final round if no other players collect all their destinations. Your party size is used as a tiebreaker if needed. 
Each player turn consists of drawing cards, playing actions (on themselves or other players), traveling to a destination, and discarding cards.

description:
A multiplayer RPS variant with an old west theme. Each round the players choose to act as a Miner (Scissors), a Bandit (Rock) or a Lawman (Paper) and earn enough money to retire. The first player to 10 Gold wins!

The Miners earn money if there is no bandit, or if there is a lawman.
The Bandits earn money for every miner, unless there is a lawman
The Lawmen earn money if there is a bandit.

2-player abstract strategy board game of movement, sliding and stacking ... that will floor you!

OBJECT:  stack three own-colour pieces on the rear-rank corner tile of the opponent.  The first player to do so, is the winner.

PLAY:  players alternately either move or leap one piece at a time to an unoccupied tile then slide an unoccupied tile to an adjacent void or, slide an unoccupied tile to an adjacent void then move or leap a piece.  A piece may not move onto or leap over a void.  Players may not slide (return) a tile to the void created immediately previous.  A player unable to move / leap a piece or slide a tile, forfeits the turn.

Compilation box including Carcassonne base game and expansions:
The River
Inns & Cathedrals

The game was packaged in a gold-colored box, and was available for only a limited time.

Rare card game that uses standard playing cards with the addition of 8 Royal Aces and eight Jokers. As there are two packs, this makes a total of 120 cards per game.

The game is a curious mix of Rummy and Whist. Each player gets 18 cards dealt to them. There now begins a phase of drawing and discarding until a player lays down a run or set of 5 cards (5 Nines or a run of five, for example). Bonuses and penalties are then scored and every player must reduce their hand to 13 cards.

Then the auction begins and one player (or team if playing partnerships) will win the auction and then need to make that many tricks as bid. Card play is exactly the same as standard games like Whist. Royal Aces secured in this phase (or Release) score sizeable bonus points.

It can be seen that strategy is needed at every phase. If you use a high scoring 5 cards in the first stage (known as Release), the cards used will not be available to you for the auction and card-play in the 2nd phase.

The object is to be the first player (or partnership) to score 1,000 points.

2-players strategy game MAIDANIA: “UA for EU vs. Dictator”

This “revolutionary-intellectual“ game  is dedicated to the so called „Euromaidan“, pro-European Union protests in Ukraine these days. 
The patriots struggle in Maidans in Kyiv and in other Ukrainian cities against the riot police "Berkut"– for justice and democracy. 
One player plays for all "berkuts" and other plays for all protesters.

You can play for the leaders of Ukrainian opposition (Yulia Tymoshenko, Vitaliy Klitschko and others) and also for “casual” Ukrainians: a doctor, a student, a driver, а worker etc.

The protesters try to reach crossword-like walls of the game board in order to place there their tents with the democratic and patriotic slogans: FREEDOM, DEMOCRACY etc.The “berkuts” try to make the protesters “silent” and “paralyzed” by jumping over them. Both "berkuts" and the protesters move in all directions but jump only vertically and horizontally.

Tooth Terror is a hilarious 2-3 player card game in which you assume the role of a particularly nasty dentist. You score 'Pain points' by performing treatments. These vary from moderately (Drilling) to excruciatingly painful (Root Canal). Try to make your treatments as painful as possible in order to collect as much Pain points as possible.

To hinder your opponents, you play cards that make their treatments less painful (Anesthesia, Sterile equipment). Of course you can also make your own treatments more painful by playing cards like Blood loss.

The game ends when there are no more cards to draw from the shared deck. Everybody compares their points and whoever has inflicted the most pain upon his patients is the winner of Tooth Terror.

This is a game of football. The rules are simple. Your objective is to score more goals than your opponent and win the match!

Coaches take turns, first the attacker, then the defender. The attacker tries to move the ball between his players and score a goal. The defender tries to get the ball.

Pick your team out of 10 national teams, challenge your friends and become a world class coach!

Objective: be the first reaching space 60 (school)
- moving by turns -
simple 'Roll & Move' game; each player rolls the die (1d6) & moves forward; ending on a red space you have to follow instructions.

In Pick Nick, each player has two or three insects that they want to move out of the garden to safety. Nick the bird, however, has other plans and will peck at exposed insects to send them scurrying back to the starting line.

To set up, players takes two insects for a short game and three for a longer game; place the leaves into their holders and set them next to the game board so that they cover certain spaces. On a turn, a player rolls the die, then adds one of his insects to the movement track. Once all of a player's insects are on the track, he moves any one of his choice on his turn based on the die roll. If he rolls Nick, he makes the bird pick up one of the insect tokens with his beak, ideally catching one of the opponents! That player must start over with that token. Whoever reaches the end of the track with all of his insects first wins.

Objective: be the first to get the permanent majority of votes for the coming election of the mayor - learn more about the city of Oldenburg
- moving/acting by turns.

Each player rolls the die, moves according the number, follows instructions of space landed on; buys, if possible and/or (preferable) votes.

"Chance" cards are created with circumstances out of the history of Oldenburg. Correct answers give economic advantages.

Voting process about the next mayor: Each owned company corresponds to a number of votes. The results of each voting are kept and the final winner is the first player who reaches the target.

Climaxxx is a Roll and move erotic game for two players with similar mechanics to Snakes and Ladders. The goal of the game is to be the first to reach the end of the board. However, you lose the game if you have an orgasm before your partner at any time before the end.

The game is played in two separate phases, the first being softer while the second phase is played naked. Throughout the game, players may have to pick a card and do what the image on it explains. Other possible areas are 'Wine/Drinks pauses' and 'Bite the other player'.

The winner gets to dare the other player to do something he or she wants.

From the publisher's website:
The game for lovers that takes you and your partner through a dizzying array of sensuous thrills. Illustrated cards guide you through a series of sexy trials. But be careful! No matter how excited you get, you have to keep your passions in check.

The game includes: - 1 board - 4 decks of cards - 1 die - 1 hourglass - 2 tokens

Players race against time to find the objects cleverly hidden in colourful illustrations that match the pictures on each card drawn. The player who collects the most cards is the winner. The game includes 6 game boards and 50 different object cards.

In the 14th century, the fiercely independent republic of Lucca became the site of bitter rivalries between warring families. But vying for political power wasn't enough. Crammed together inside the city walls, Lucca's noble families built great towers--some spent their entire fortunes--jostling with one another to reach the tallest heights and gain the perfect view of the Tuscan countryside. Build towers to maximize your View of the countryside around Lucca while obstructing the Views of your opponents. The game ends when all of Lucca has been filled, and the player with the most total View wins.

Save your life through knowledge of traffic signs! The players have to go from "Start" to the finish space. If the meeples get on a space with a traffic sign, the players have to follow the instructions in the game rules. On a few occations the player may turn the turntable to shorten his way. The first player to reach "Ziel" wins the game.

The game was published in the 1950s.

Set Up:
Place the a ring on a flat surface and put a column in each of the recesses. When each recess is occupied place another ring on top and fill in the same way. Alternate the column colours as you complete each ring. Repeat this process until all the columns have been used, you will be left with 4 spare rings.

Game Play:
- The youngest player starts the game, play then continues in a clockwise direction.
- On each go, players throw the coloured die. They then have to remove a column with the same colour as the die, from any ring except the top.
- All removed columns are replaced on the top ring of the tower, when all the recesses to the top ring are full a new ring is added.
- If the topples during a player's turn they are eliminated from the game, the tower is rebuilt and play continues with the remaining players.
- The winner of the game is the last player in play!

Objective:
The object of the game is to be the last player to skilfully remove the columns from the lower levels of the tower to the top, without making the tower topple.

Contents: 
11 rings, 60 columns, game die.

Courage is a squad based wartime strategy game where players vie for control of the battlefield using custom troops, secret orders, and subterfuge. Acting simultaneously, 2-8 players take on the role of Axis or Allied forces and engage in anything from small skirmishes to epic team battles. Courage emphasizes the use of tactics and troop management over luck to win the battlefield.

The game of Courage is an accessible yet deep and intuitive strategy game designed for fast paced simultaneous play that keeps all players in the action, every turn.

Zeppeldrome: Faceoff is set in the same steam punk world as its multi-player namesake, Zeppeldrome. It is a serious race through silly obstacles for two-players, with a hazardous board and cards that are used to help you or mess with your opponent as you both race to be the first to travel the length of the airship raceway.

The first player over the finish line is the winner!

The boards one center piece contains a mixture of hazards. Additionally, 2/3 of the deck are variants of Zeppeldrome's cards, but the remaining 1/3 are all new.

You are a citizen of the town of Bastion.  Your town guard have been slain by barbarians.  And now they are coming for you.  Your job is to defend your town.  You are no great warrior.  You are no mighty soldier.  You are just a normal citizen.  But you are also Bastion's only hope.

The Last Bastion is a competitive game for 1-4 players.  You goal is to train enough with the village elders to be able to save your town, then go out and defeat the barbarian horde.  Train too much and the horde will overrun the town.  Train too little and you will be trampled.  Will you be the hero of Bastion, or it's martyr.

JAM is a card game for two coaches, or teams, based around the Movement Programming mechanic.

Caverna: Christmas Chamber is a postcard expansion for the Caverna board game that consists of a single tile in both English and German. With the tile, for 1 wood you can furnish a cavern for Christmas and earn 4 points at the end of the game.
