description:
Mystique Guilds is a 2 to 4 player game for The Mystique Deck derived from Nestortiles Duel, by the same designer. You need one Mystique Deck to play the game.
 In Mystique Guilds, the combination of suit+colour is called ‘guild’. So there are 12 guilds (3 colours times 4 suits). Your goal is to control the most guilds when the game ends.

Shuffle the whole deck and place it facing down in the middle of the table. This is the Draw Deck. Next to it a (now inexistent) Discard Deck will be created.

Determine the starting player by any peaceful means. Players take turns in anticlockwise order during the game until the endgame condition is reached.

On their turn, players must do only one of these 2 actions:

- Pick one card from the Draw Deck or the Discard Deck.

- Buy cards.

How to buy cards:

1. Place one of your cards facing up before you. This is the Influence Card.

2. Draw up to as many cards from the Draw Deck as the number on your Influence Card.

3. Place the Influence Card facing up on the Discard Deck.

If, at any moment, the Draw Deck gets exhausted, the game ends.

Each player adds up the values of each guild on their hand. The player with the highest value for each guild controls that guild and scores a point (tip: score the 12 guilds in order for clarity). In case of two or more players having the same value for a guild, none of them scores for it. The guild of blue suns is an exception and scores two points. So there are 13 available points in total.

Ties for the first place are broken by the lowest number of cards. If a tie persists then play again.

Variants:

Variant 1: Cards can be bought from both decks.

Variant 2 (the Market): You can buy cards from another player. Both players must agree on the cards being exchanged. The Influence card is given to that player instead of discarding it.

Risk and Castle Risk in one box.

Castle Risk

Each player starts out with one castle on the game board. Your objective is to capture all your opponents' castles, so that you are the last player remaining in the game.

This game in the Risk family of games is markedly different than the original Risk. The combat mechanism is the same but players receive armies at the end of the turn, not the beginning (the sole exception being playing a Reinforcements card). The game has no territory cards, and one does not get armies based on the total number of individual territories. One gets armies for regions controlled and banners in one's castle only. Each player starts with a single banner in his castle but also takes the banners of any captured castles.

The cards act as die modifiers (General and Marshall), allow attacks one would not normally be able to make (Admiral), get armies at the start of a turn (Reinforcements), force temporarily non-aggression pacts (Diplomats) and look at another player's cards, discarding one in the process (Spy). The Spy card can also be used to defend against another Spy.

Risk

Players are given army units to place onto the map of the world. Players use units to attack other players' positions, hopefully with superior numbers. Combat is a simple dice rolling affair that stresses attrition, and reinforcements are given to players who collect sets of cards.

From the box:

Europe is your battleground and enemy castles your target in this thrilling game of military strategy. Your tactics range from highly planned to hit-and-run, as you deploy your troops: Choose from Spies, Generals and Diplomats to carry out your battle plans. Call out your Marshalls, Reinforcements, the Admiral, and, just once in the game, your Hidden Armies. Now roll the dice to see who wins each battle.

boardgame: Madagascar (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description:
This Madagascar map should offer similar play to the US editions. It is intended for 2 to 4 players.


NOTE: "Ticket to Ride" and "Days of Wonder" are registered trademarks of Days of Wonder, Inc. This fan-created expansion map is not a Days of Wonder product, but has been created with permission of Days of Wonder, Inc, solely for unpaid, non-commercial use. The Kingswood is a tile placement and role choosing game.

You play as a great merchant house, trying to build roads - or pave over existing ones - to gain gold from the treasure at the heart of the Kingswood.  You will also attempt to call on a number of professions to help you with your task. Gain the best routes, while also avoid competing too heavily for the same professions and paths, and you'll be able to gain enough gold to buy the deed of the Kingswood and win.

The crux of the game is connecting to the most lucrative treasure gates by laying down road tiles - even on other road tiles already on the board.  
One well placed road tile can switch the flow of gold from your opponents to you. Subverting the paths of your rivals is vital - as is reducing the ability for your opponents to do the same.

Each round, you choose 1 of 5 Roles to help you dominate The Kingswood: Merchant, Real Estate Agent, Foreman, Soldier and Spy. Each Role has specific abilities to help you get ahead, as well as determining the order players play in for the round. You must be careful with your choice. If you choose the same Role as another, your bickering will prevent both of you from using the Role to its full potential.

Once a player has 50 or more gold at the end of the round, the game is over.  If you have the most gold, you buy the deed to The Kingswood and win.

boardgame: The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – Game Night Kit 2014 Season One
description:
The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game - Game Night Kit 2014 Season One includes 3 nightmare decks as one 60-card deck, "Over Hill and Under Hill":


    We Must Away Ere Break of Day
    Over the Misty Mountains Grim
    Dungeons Deep and Caverns Dim


Game Night Kits are technically only available to retailers and not for general purchase. Game Night Kits contain one 60-card Nightmare Deck, Tournament rules, a Poster and a Goodie (Playmat, Deck box). It is a limited release, which is meant to support FFG's Game Night program.

The 60-card deck was later sold as The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – Nightmare Decks: Over Hill and Under Hill.

description: This promotional Survivor is available as a prize for the map/scenario design contest sponsored by Guillotine Games, which can be found on their website. A zombie version (Zombivor) is also available.

Boardgame for 2 – 4 children (Ages 6 to 10) in which they have to manage their pocket money. Thematically based on a fun-fair visit during the holidays the players have to spend their money on various items. But with their limited amount of money they can't afford everything they want - they have to earn some money. Only if they are able to manage their pocket money successfully will they win the game.

Gameplay:
The players start with a given amount of pocket money and move across the board rolling one dice (d6). Movement is allowed in any direction and players may move less spaces than their dice roll. On special card fields they are able to fulfill six actions (spending money on several items and earning money to afford them) which are needed to win the game. The placement of the action cards is strictly ruled - making following-up cards harder to buy things (prices will rise) and harder to earn money (earnings will decrease). After each players' turn the turn marker is moved one day further. When the 7th turn ends (= one week) all players receive a new amount of pocket money.

The first player who has fulfilled all his actions, wins. If there is a tie, the player with most of the cash wins.

description:
In the late 2013 the Italian gaming club Dinx celebrated its tenth anniversary: to commemorate the event, the club gave every active member a tumbler and three customized engraved dice, whose 6-pane was replaced with the club logo.

Dinx & Dragons has been designed as a mini-game to be played with these dice. It can also be played with regular d6s.

About the game

In Dinx & Dragons players are red dragons fighting each other by claws and fangs: by rolling dice, players try to either hit the opponent or parry his blows.

Each time an attack is successful, that player steals (and possibly eats) a candy from the opponent’s pool, thus reducing his hit points.

As a player runs out of candies, he’s out of the game: the last player with candies in his pool is the winner.

"Italia '44" is a two player wargame simulation (solitaire play is also possible, but without a specific integrated Solitaire rules system) of the Italian Campaign from the Anzio landing to the first assaults against the Gothic Line (January-October 1944).

90 counters, division-brigade scale, 20 km/hex. The map shows central Italy, from the Berhardt Line to Bologna and the Gothic Line.

The game uses a simplified version of the Hungary 44-45 rules system published in Vae Victis #78.

description:
Each player gets 4 different meeples of one color: man, woman, letter and luggage. The movements are determined by a special roulette wheel. The goal is to travel from the starting spacees (1-4) to the summer vacation spots (A-D). The 1st player to get all 4 meeples to his vacation spot, wins the game.

Each spinning of the roulette wheel determines the moves of all 4 players (A-D) at once. For the different kind of meeples, there are different rules of how and when they move and what happens, when the meet each other. So for example, the woman can carry a man of any color (this symbolizes them forming a pair) and the letter causes man and woman to step a few spaces back, while they are busy with reading. Some encounters even cause the meeples to get sent back to their home to start anew.

This game most likely was published in the 1930s.

An 1830 mini-game for 3 to 6 players designed by AICS LABS.

To play 1850 jr you need a copy of the original 1830 game from Avalon Hill. All 1830 rules are in effect, with some modifications.

The goal of the game is to become the richest robber baron, and also to play a shorter game with the same full flavour of 1830.

The game is set in the island of Sicily, and the map is smaller than 1830.

Published with the Italian fanzine Bollettino T.

description:
Bedtime Heroes is a card and dice game where players try to protect sleeping children from nightmarish monsters with the aid of animated toys.

During a turn players may try to add to their toy collection by purchasing one through silent auction, or they may battle monsters in a bid to earn points.

The game ends when all monsters are defeated - points are earned by defeating monsters, having a big toy collection, and making sure your child is still fast asleep!

description:
The French and Indian War battlepack is Bill Molyneaux's conversion of our AWI ruleset to this earlier conflict. Although it is far more detailed than the AWI sets, especially the unit differentiation, it is still easy to learn.

Most of the 12 battles can be finished in under 2 hours regardless of your wargaming experience. Experienced players will comfortably finish most battles in under an hour.

The battle scenarios included in this campaign pack are:-
Jumonville Glen
Fort Neccessity
Braddocks Defeat
The Mary Jemison raid
Lake George
Sideling Hill
Fort William Henry
Snowshoes
Carillon
Plains of Abraham
Sainte Foy
Bushy Run

description:
In Battlesculpt, 2 - 8 players make a few simple choices, then create a monster out of modeling clay to do battle until only one monster remains.

Each monster has a Special Ability and a Monster Tracking Board with three columns of choices. Grab a card, choose one option from each column, sculpt your clay, and decimate your opponents.

When your monster has been squished don't worry you can still flail at any monsters who stay near your lump of smashed clay as well as throw missiles at the still mobile combatants.

In Creature Dice, players take on the role of a creature from myth and legend trying to gather victims and avoid the Monster Hunters.

As each player rolls dice to hunt, other players may join the hunt and score based on the dice kept by the active player. Keeping all players engaged regardless of whose turn it is.

Each player will be dealt a monster card which shows the types of victims it can hunt.

On your turn, you will roll all eight custom dice. You are allowed to keep as many of these dice as you'd like, but you must keep at least one.

Your first hunt (roll) is safe. If you roll three hunters on this roll, you still score all victims of your type and your turn ends immediately. No other players may score on this first roll.

If you roll fewer than 3 hunters, you may continue rolling all non-hunter dice you did not choose to hold. On this second roll and any subsequent rolls, other players score if you hold a die with their victim type on it. If at any time after your first roll you roll a total of three hunters, then your turn ends immediately and you score zero. All other players may still score on your roll, unless lock them out with a special die result that can ban them from collecting victims on your hunt.

Players' victim types overlap so that multiple players may score on the same die.

The last round begins when any player reaches the Victim (victory) Point goal (5 points per player) and the player with the most points at the end of this last round wins the game.

boardgame: The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – The Three Trials
description:
Game description from the publisher:

After their efforts to help Saruman discover the means to forestall the forces of Mordor leads them deep into the heart of Dunland, several of Middle-earth's greatest heroes must undertake a series of three deadly trials.

The Three Trials is the second Adventure Pack in "The Ring-maker" cycle for The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game. In its new scenario, can Middle-earth's heroes defeat three ferocious Dunlending Guardian spirits, claim their Keys, and recover an ancient Dunlending artifact, the Antlered Crown? Additionally, players receive a new hero and three copies of each player card, including two new Elven allies and support for the game's Secrecy keyword.

description:
The King is dead! Long live the…who??? Amidst the power vacuum left by the King’s untimely demise, the Royal Courtesans are all salivating over the claim to the high seat of the regency throne. Secret coalitions are banded, Schemes are plotted, and good ol’ daggers are stabbed in the back. Play your hand of Royals in a game of Dominance assertion, all the while screwing your competitor. The crown is within your grasp; you just need to step over others to get it!

Royals is a micro card game that is quick to pick up, but hard to put down. The rules are simple. Each player draws a set of cards, and must utilize them in the right combination to net them the most points.

But not so fast! For you can play each card in two ways - place it for points, or use its schemes for quick, chain comboing actions. This means there are bizzllions of ways that you could play your hand! Your perfect plan could easily be broken by other’s schemes, and a risky draw might be all that you need to score big!

So grab some buddies or kiddies, start up a game of Royals, and see who end up sitting on that throne!

Victory Decision: Future Combat is a comprehensive game system to using SF Infantry units and Armoured Fighting Vehicles in Victory Decision. These rules cover almost all aspects of Science Fiction wargaming, from Troop Quality and Leadership to Air Strikes, Bunker Assaults, Combat in Buildings, Psi Powers, Vehicle and VTOL combat.

Victory Decision: Future Combat features a unique Leadership-modified alternate unit activation system with an integrated Electronic Warfare aspect. The result is a fun and fast paced war game that involves both players constantly.

The rules cover individually based infantry miniatures and also group- or multibased miniatures, aka Infantry Stands. While designed with 20-28 mm miniatures in mind the Victory Decision game system is flexible enough to handle any miniatures from 6 mm to 32 mm. So whatever your existing miniature collection consists of, you can start playing right away.

This rulebook not only provides 8 generic Scenarios but also point based Force lists that can be used to represent many classic SF settings. All necessary Game Markers are also included within this book.

Goal: Be the first to race to position '140'
Typical 'Roll & Move' game

Roll the 2 dice by turns and step forward - until you end on a red marked space follow instructions (lid inside)

Notes:
Likely published 1910/20s
Nice pawns

Objective: be the first reaching the target (field 1) after having finished 2 circles

‘Roll and Move’ game

Ending on a marked space (red/black) follow instructions glued into the lid

Equipment: board, 6 meeples (cars), 1 die

Goal: be the first completing your matrix with cards (from different eras/continent)
acting simultaneously - operate one of the spinners (era or continent); all play appropriate cards - player with most cards collects all.
Barricades can be used to block one opponent;  
turn one of the spinners...

Contents:
rule & instruction sheet (about the eras), board, board with 2 spinners, 6 matrices, ~320 cards, 2 barricades

Can you detect which suspects are the mathological liars? This card game is based on fun mini math mysteries. Each round of card play is a new case. Players are each dealt a suspect card. There are 4 suspects per case, one or more who may be guilty. One player reads aloud the case summary. Then, all players silently read their suspect’s alibi. If the math in their suspect’s alibi is correct, then he is innocent. Incorrect math indicates guilt. Players decide if their suspect is innocent or guilty and later take turns vocalizing their verdict and mathematical reasoning. Points are awarded based on correct determination of a suspect’s innocence or guilt and also for describing why the guilty party’s alibi did not jive (i.e., why the math was incorrect). The winner is the player with the most points after a pre-determined number of rounds or time limit set at the beginning of game play. You’ll need to draw on a variety of math skills to solve these whodunit mysteries as each game includes 50 different cases.

A ring toss game - 
The gamepieces are a face with a nose and 2 hooks, 8 rings (2 x red, blue, green, yellow).

The players toss rings and try to hook them on clown's nose which sticks out from the face (counts '10'). If you miss the nose and hit a hook instead (left and right side) you score a 2 or 3 points). The first player to accumulate 50 Points is declared the winner.

Instructions are printed on the back of the lid.

Notes:

     Rules are written in rhyme
     Likely published 1910/20's

Typical spin and move game.
Rules of the game: each player has four Scouts opposite color.. After deciding who starts first spin top and advance one Scout as many foot-prints as top indicates. Opponent then spins and Advances one Scout starting from opposite camp. Enter additional Scouts when you want to in your turn. One Scout in a square can be sent back to start over should opponent spin and land in that square. Two Scouts in a square is a guard and cannot be passed by opponent Scouts. 
Friendly camp is neutral ground and is a safety zone. You may reach friendly-camp or finish by getting number large enough to reach but it is not necessary to throw 'exact number. It is advisable to form a guard wherever possible as opponent must wait to pass until you have broken your guard. Player who gets all his Scouts in opponent's camp first wins the game.

Two different games (board can be turned). Before players start they have to 'buy in' (money will be divided between first (1/2), second (1/3) and third (1/6) place).

1. Tourenrennen - moving by turns, be the first reaching space 90. Simple 'Roll & Move' game - ending on a marked space follow instructions (lid inside).

2. Wettrennen - acting/moving simultaneously/agree upon number of rounds. All players throw their die and move forward; ending on a coloured space (green/red/black/blue/yellow/purple) follow instructions (lid inside)

Notes:
Likely published at the end of the 1920s.

Zeppelin Conquest is a fun 2-player card game in which two evil masterminds from the Spirit of the Century Role-Playing game: Rocket Red and Princess Cyclone, duke it out across the globe using their soldiers and zeppelins to claim an inevitably short-lived victory as queen of the world! The game utilizes a distinctive majority-control mechanic, which requires players to add set combinations of majority control resources against 3 different targets with each play.

Monsters & Knights is a casually competitive fantasy themed table top card game for 2-6 players. 
Players compete to slay the most monsters or eat the most knights.

To summarize the game play: There are two ways to play monsters and knights, casual mode, and advanced mode.

In the casual mode of monsters and knights, each round players will play all the cards in their hand, using monsters/knights to eat/slay other monsters and knights already in play. At the end of the round they will add up the points for their slayed monsters, or eaten knights and continue to play rounds until one player or team reaches a set number of points.

The advanced mode of monsters and knights adds additional game play mechanics enhancing the level of strategy for players looking for a more complex game.

This game is an ancestor of Whist, played in England in the 16th and 17th century.

The game is a trick taking card game played in partnerships and using a standard deck of cards. 12 cards are dealt to each players and the remaining 4 cards become the stock, the top of which is flipped to determine Trump. The holder of the Ace of Trump "ruffs", taking in the Stock and discarding four cards.

Each team scores one point for each trick they have in excess of 6. The game ends when a team reaches 9 points.

This game, which is very popular in India and Pakistan, has several names. The name Court Piece is sometimes written as Coat Piece or Coat Pees, Pees being a Hindi word meaning to deal. In Pakistan this game is often known as Rang, which means trump. In some places, for example in Goa, it is called Seven Hands: in India the English word "hand" is sometimes used to mean a trick - i.e. a set of cards, one played by each player in turn.

Players and Cards
There are four players in fixed partnerships, partners sitting opposite. Deal and play are anticlockwise.
The dealer deals a batch of five cards to each player. The trump-caller player looks at his or her five cards and chooses and announces the trump suit. Then the dealer deals out all the remaining cards in batches of four, so that everyone has 13 cards.

Winning
The object of the game is to score courts by winning the majority of the tricks. The team that wins at least seven of the thirteen tricks (hands) wins the deal, and a team that wins seven deals in succession scores a court.It is also possible to score a court in a single deal by winning the first seven tricks, while the opposing team scores none. The overall winners of the session are the team that has scored most courts after an agreed length of time.

Variations
Some play that the card indicating the trump suit is kept face down, so that the players other than the trump caller do not know what the trump suit will be.

Some play that the trump indicator card is selected at random from the first player's first five cards without looking, so that even this player does not know what the trump suit will be until the card is revealed.

Double Sir
Double Sir or Double Sar is a variant of Court Piece: the word sir (sar) means trick (hand). An online implementation of this variation can be found here: http://runng.com

The deal, choice of trumps and rules of play are the same as in Court Piece, but in this variant, a player who wins a  trick does not gather in the cards, but turns the cards of the trick face down in the centre of the table. Cards are only gathered in when the same player wins two consecutive tricks. Until then the tricks pile up in centre.

When a player does win two consecutive tricks, that player takes all the cards from the centre (the trick just won and the pile of previous tricks), adds them to his team's face down trick pile, and leads to the next trick. Scoring is then similar to Court Piece.

Variants of Double Sir: Be-ranga Double Sar 
In this variant the dealer deals all thirteen cards and the play begins without trumps until some player is unable to follow suit. As soon as a player cannot follow suit, the suit of the card they play instead becomes trump for the rest of the deal. No tricks can be collected until the trick after the one in which trumps are determined.

Call your friends and keep them on the line to earn points and win the game. Roll the die to reveal your prank call category and your next prank call. Then stick the phone on speaker, take a deep breath, flip the timer and start dialing. Example of a challenge: before time runs out, get your caller to repeat the word 'Circus' 3 times. First player to collect five cards wins the game.

Contents: 100 cards, timer, die, rules.

The game consists of 9 dice which show "E" on two sides, "N" on two sides, "T" on one side and "O" on the last side. The active player rolls all the dice and forms the words "ten" and "one" from the results. For each "ten", s/he may put aside a "T" (counting 10 points), for each "one", s/he may put aside an "O" (counting one point). The remaining dice may be re-rolled as many times as desired, but when a player can form neither form a "ten" nor a "one", s/he loses all the points from the current turn. 
The first player to reach 200 points is the winner.

Introduction
Omi is a popular card game played in Sri Lanka: in some regions it is known as Oombi. Omi is a straightforward 4-player partnership plain-trick game of the Whist type, somewhat similar to the Indian and Pakistani game Court Piece in that the first player has to choose trumps on the basis of a the first few cards. The game is played using 32 cards of a standard deck, the other cards being used as tokens for scoring.

Play
The player to dealer's right, who announced the trump suit, leads any card to the first trick. The other players play in turn, anticlockwise around the table, and must follow suit if able to; a player who holds no card of the suit led may play any card.

Scoring
After the eighth trick, the tricks won by each team are counted. Depending on the number of tricks, a certain amount of tokens are earned. The winners are the first team to win 10 or more tokens.

Introduction
Satat is the most popular card game in Mauritius. Its name comes from the from the Hindi "saat hant" meaning "seven hands" and it is related to the Indian game of Seven Hands (also known as Court Piece). The objective is to win at least seven of the 13 tricks, or even better to win the first 7 tricks, or better still all 13 tricks.

Players and Cards
The game plays with four players in fixed partnerships using a standard 52-card pack. What distinguish Satat from other Whist-like trick tacking games are the fact that the 2 of heart is the highest trump card of the game and the ways trump is decided.

Making Trumps
The dealer shuffles, the player to dealer's right cuts, and the dealer deals a batch of five cards to each player. The trump maker, to dealer's left, looks at these five cards and announces the trump suit. Alternatively, if unwilling to decide trumps on the basis of these first five cards, the trump maker may elect to use the last four cards to determine the trump suit.

End Game and Scoring
Normally the play ends when one team wins by taking seven tricks. However, if a team wins the first seven tricks (a 7-0 win) they may continue the play and try to win all the tricks (13-0 win). The winning team then gets to pass cards to the losing team in exchange for trump cards in a way similar to Sergeant Major.

There is no formal system of scoring and no fixed length to the game, but the players may like to keep track of how many games each team has won or lost.

Variations

Trup kasiet
In this "hidden trump" variation, instead of announcing the trump suit, the trump maker places one card of the trump suit face down. If the trump card is selected unseen from the last four, the trump maker looks at it before placing it face down.

In this variation 7-0 and 13-0 wins are more frequent, but no cards are exchanged after a 7-0 or 13-0 win.

Arpaa Turup is a four-player plain trick taking card game from Somalia, played in partnerships, whose name means "Four [packs of] Cards". No doubt it is so called because it is played with a 144-card pack made up four identical 36-card packs.

Cards in this game are dealt in batch of three. After the player sitting on the dealer's left gets the first three cards, the dealing is stopped and this player, having looked at his cards, calls the trump suit.

As usual the highest trump, or if none are played the highest card of the suit led wins the trick.  The winner of each trick leads to the next.

Winning the game and the match
At the end of the game each partnership counts the tricks they have taken. The game is won by the party which takes more tricks. To win the match, a partnership must win two games, without losing any games in between them, the number of tricks taken in the second game they win being more than in the first game they win.

Game description from the publisher:

Upstalk is the first Data Pack in the "Lunar Cycle". With its sixty new cards (three copies each of twenty different cards), Upstalk transports Android: Netrunner to an all-new meat space: the moon, where life is one-point-two seconds out of the ordinary. Here, the game defies standard expectations. Runners and Corps explore new strategies, as do many of the game's cards and mechanisms. Here, Corporate trace attempts can punish Runners even when they fail. Corporate assets are everywhere, there's no life outside of Corporate-sponsored air, and there's a chance that those who manage or run the net may encounter a shard or fragment of the mythical source code. What can happen in one-point-two seconds? Everything!
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)

description:
The Nile Ran Red is a collection of three standalone card games set in a mythical ancient Egypt. Illustrated by John Ariosa, these card games all pit players against each other with a common goal: extending control over the land of the Nile. While all three games share a common theme and a common aesthetic, they are vastly different mechanically and no games share common components or art. With this collection of games, up to eleven players can be accommodated at once — albeit by playing different games.

•••

In Lords of the Sand, each player takes on the role of one of four different gods: Anubis, Thoth, Sobek, and Khnum. Each god has its own unique agenda and special abilities.

Throughout the course of the game, players use Units, complete their court, use special abilities, and build temple complexes. With a combo-driven card system, as cards are played, additional actions may spawn multiple abilities, but a limited action system keeps downtime to a minimum.

Lords of the Sand offers multiple paths to victory, unique card play, and various special abilities with a playing time of about thirty minutes, plus ten minutes for each player above two.

•••

In Rise of the First Dynasty, 3-5 players vie for control over the whole of Egypt as the potential leader of the mythical first Dynasty of the ancient land along the Nile.

Each player has a set of three identical actions. Played over ten Aeons (or rounds), in each Aeon each player chooses one of his action cards. Depending on the actions chosen by each other players, the player will gain actions, such as conquering new lands, blessing the lands he has already conquered, or using Edict cards to thwart the other players' plans and protect his own. Light, quick, and downright dirty, Rise of the First Dynasty plays in around 30 minutes and scales well regardless of players.

•••

In Crimson Sun, two players take on the role of leaders of Upper and Lower Egypt. Playing units of four unique gods (Kuk, Babi, Horus, and Sekmet), players build their forces and attempt to assert influence over four key structures. Players may choose to either draft decks at the beginning of the game, or use a single deck.

During the game, players use the Unit cards in a variety of ways to increase his overall strength, to use special abilities, or to secretly try to gain control over the structures. The core mechanisms of this game are pulled from a previous Small Box Games release — Bhazum — but mechanical and thematic changes have lead to a game that should be a fresh experience for players already familiar with the earlier game.

Pleiades, an early adaptation of dominoes to triangular pieces, was granted a patent in 1879.  The game consists of a central six-pointed star piece, each point being marked on both outer edges with a number of dots from one to six, and thirty-six triangular tiles marked along their edges with numbers ranging from one to six.

To begin the game, the tiles are placed face down and shuffled, and two or more players draw an agreed upon number.  The player who has drawn a tile with three sixes goes first, matching a tile to one of the central star's numbered edges.  The players then take turns matching their tiles to the original in order to create a hexagon shaped star adjoining the central piece.  If no match is possible, a player must pass.  The player who completes the hexagonal star gets one point, and the next player begins another star by matching a tile to the central star.  If no player can match a piece, that star is blocked and play begins on another.  The player scoring the most points (there are a maximum of six) wins the game.

In Rise of the First Dynasty, 3-5 players vie for control over the whole of Egypt as the potential leader of the mythical first Dynasty of the ancient land along the Nile.

Each player has a set of three identical actions. Played over ten Aeons (or rounds), in each Aeon each player chooses one of his action cards. Depending on the actions chosen by each other player, the player will gain actions, such as conquering new lands, blessing the lands he has already conquered, or using Edict cards to thwart the other players' plans and protect his own. Light, quick, and downright dirty, Rise of the First Dynasty plays in around 30 minutes and scales well regardless of players.

The Kit is a collection of Expansionary items for Police Precinct.

There have been several variations of "The Kit" over the years.  The original Kit was designed for owners of the 1st Edition, then came one that focused on the needs of the 2nd Edition owners, and several versions have come since.

Basically the Kit grows with each new expansionary item that is released (as it is added to the package).

FULL MONTY:
Another type of Kit came out in early 2016 known as "The Full Monty".  This one was (and still is) designed to help owners of the 1st Edition to Upgrade toward the 2nd Edition by swapping-out certain components:
The board (This is the 2nd Edition Board which includes The Unterbelly)
All of the Characters (This is the 2nd Edition Character array which includes 8 new characters and perfect gender balance)
All of the cards (both the larger cards and smaller, including the very popular 2nd Edition Event Deck)
The rulebook (The book supplied with The Full Monty is the Revised 1st Edition Rulebook which was the inspiration and template for the 2nd Edition book)
As an added bonus, the Full Monty also includes a copy of Crooked Lawyers.

STILL IN PRINT:
To see the versions of "The Kit" that are still in print from Common Man Games, visit the CMG website.  Specifically this page...
www.commonman.com/2e

EARLY VERSIONS:
Looking back in time, here are the first two versions of the Kit:

1st Edition Kit INCLUDES:
The Heat (mini-expansion)
Rioting Gangs (Booster-Pack)
Mad Men 5-Pack (Booster-Pack, minus one card of the original 6-pack)
Promo Cards (Rocket Launcher & Dirty Bomb)
And...
The Revised Rulebook

2nd Edition Kit INCLUDES:
The Heat (mini-expansion)
Rioting Gangs (Booster-Pack)
Mad Men 5-Pack (Booster-Pack, minus one card of the original 6-pack)
Promo Cards (Rocket Launcher & Dirty Bomb)
This Kit is exactly like the 1st Edition Kit except that it does NOT have a Revised Rulebook (because the 2nd Edition will come stocked with the 2nd Edition rulebook).

“Anyone who owns Police Precinct should own the Kit too!”

A card and dice based game set in Jaime Buckley’s world of Wanted Hero.

Each player begins with a random character and weapon, and has a different quest to fulfill. In each turn they visit different locations, where they may fight fierce foes, escape terrible traps, discover wondrous treasures, trade their findings, or recruit additional characters.

Their ability to escape the perilous traps or win their opposing foes is determined by their own strength, the strength of their weapon... and a bit of luck from the dice roll.

They play until they collect all the pieces needed to complete their quest and race to take them to “Til Thorin” to win the game.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

boardgame: Devine qui vient dîner ce soir
description: Guess Who’s Coming to Dinner is a fun and zany party game in which players try to arrange celebrities at restaurant tables. Due to the fact that the cards become obsolete in a few years, if not months, and that a different version would be needed not only for each language, but for each country, it will never be published, so I have published it as a downloadable pdf on this website. Of course, the cards are for a French version, but if anyone finds the idea fun enough and wants to make a US version (or a version for any other country), he is welcome to do it.

The Myth: Crawlers Minion Pack consists of ten crawler minions identical to those included in the base Myth game. These crawlers provide additional enemies that can be used to harass the heroes and spread the influence of the darkness.

The Myth: Grubbers Minion Pack consists of ten grubber minions identical to those included in the base Myth game. Grubbers are brutes that specialize in melee combat, and with more grubbers in play, it's easier to harass the heroes and spread the influence of the darkness.

THEME: 
Based on the fantasy novel TRENCH WARS, players are gnome pilots about to engage in the championship finals of an extreme sport called G.E.A.R.S. (Gnomes Engaging And Recovering the Standard). Players are walked through the last moments before the bay doors open, connecting to the mainframe computer and assigning the positions of your R.A.T.s (Recon Attack Team). A high-tech feel is produced through bullet holes, digital accents and blue metal artwork, both across the game board and cards.

GAME PLAY:
Players take turns moving their team (consisting of one Champion and four R.A.T.s) to the center of the board. R.A.T.s are divided into melee and ranged fighters, each with unique and limited moving patterns and attack abilities. Your Champion may move one space in any direction and is the only character who can complete the game by grabbing the Standard.

R.A.T.s engage and run interference while your Champion navigates the battlefield. Power cards and specialty cards provide unique abilities, buffs, advantages and pitfalls which can enhance your ability to harm or hinder opponents. Fellow players can also engage in combat, assisting either attacking or defending players by discarding cards from their hands.

GOAL:
The focus is to get your Champion to the Standard first.

description:
THEME:
The game is based on a comical aspect pulled from Jaime Buckley's "Chronicles of a Hero" series--the animated smiley face on the main character's shirt. Always revealing the true feelings of Wendell through silent expressions, this concept was applied to the comical facial expressions of the game cards.

GAME PLAY:
Based on the traditional GO FISH! rules (both US and AUSTRALIAN versions), players must ask for cards by mimicking the facial expressions contained on their card. If the player being asked does not have the expression asked for, they respond by saying "GO Smiley!" and a card is drawn. Traditional rules apply.

GOAL:
The game continues until either someone has no cards left in their hand or the stock runs out. The winner is the player who then has the most books.

GO Smiley! is also used as a memory game for children

The game is named after the Swedish version of the Eurovision song contest. The players are divided into teams. Each teams playing piece advance along a track which determines their action on a turn. The track is shaped as as a spiral with the goal at the top.

The actions about the Eurovision song contest, are provided on a DVD, and can be Guess movie clip, Guess person, Sing song, Know the song text, Know facts. In case of correct answers you advance one or two steps on the track, and the aim is to reach the top first.

During the game your team can also be prompted to take a random event card. These cards can for instance double the points received.

Content:
1 Playing board
2 Playing pieces
9 Event cards 
1 DVD
1 Rule booklet

Tribello is a trick-taking game of the Whist type for three players, using a standard 52-card deck.

Four hands of 13 cards are dealt - one for each player and a spare hand called the pickup.

There are four rounds of play with three deals per round. In the first three rounds the object is to win tricks; in the fourth the aim is to avoid winning tricks. The rounds are as follows. Each player has a quota of tricks. Each round, the way trumps are chosen is different.

In the first three rounds, the dealer has a quota of six tricks, the person to the left of the dealer has a quota of four tricks, and the person to the right of the dealer has a quota of three tricks. If you take more than your quota you score one point per extra trick; if you take less than your quota you score minus one point per trick below quota.

In the fourth round, the dealer has a quota of three tricks, the person to the left of the dealer has a quota of four tricks, and the person to the right of the dealer has a quota of six tricks. If you take more that your quota you score minus one point for each surplus trick; if you take less than your quota you score plus one for each trick below.

For each round, after the players have looked at their hands, the dealer decides how many cards he wants from the pickup (zero to 13). He discards this number of cards from his hand, and then draws an equal number from the top of the pickup, which remains face down. If there are cards remaining in the pickup, the process is repeated with other players.

The winner is the player with the most points at the end of the fourth round.

Manni is an Icelandic trick-taking card game and has a number of local versions, such as Trjámann, Hornafjarðarmanni and Laugavatnsmanni. "Manni" means little man or chap and is the name given to the spare hand of cards dealt to the table, which may be used to improve a player's hand.

In the game, players receive a hand of cards at the start of a round, with some cards being dealt to the table to form the manni; one or more players can swap cards with the manni based on various rules depending on the exact version of the game. Each round is played under a "contract" — no trumps, the suit of the cut card as trump, avoid taking tricks — and players who do well in the contract score points, with player completing a certain number of rounds or playing until someone reaches an agreed-upon point threshold.

This 18th century game is about the simplest two-player adaptation of Whist that can be imagined.

This game uses a standard 52-card pack.

The dealer deals all the cards one at a time, turning the last card face up. The suit of this card, which belongs to the dealer, is the trump suit for the hand.

The Play
The aim is to win tricks. Each trick consists of two cards, one played by each player. The first player plays any card and the second player must play a card of the same suit if possible. In this case the higher card wins the trick.

When all the cards have been played, the player who won more tricks wins in proportion to the difference in tricks between the players - for example 8 units if the winner has 17 tricks and the loser 9.

Designed as an exclusive promo for Unpub4. The Compounded Methamphetamine compound was made for those clamoring for an expansion based on a wildly popular television show. This is probably the closest to that becoming a reality.

The Methamphetamine Compound can be used as a Lab Partner card, following the same rules for that expansion (which is included in all copies of the base game).

Players stack colored "neck bones" onto the neck of a 3D dinosaur. But when a color's not available, the player must knock one "neck bone" of that color off with the hammer. Be careful because one false move and the neck will snap, sending all the "neck bones" flying, and you lose the game.

A free Print and Play Table Top Survival Horror War Game, it contains three different games in one: A Fantasy War Game, a Modern War Game and a role playing pen and paper version.

Features: Point based customizable armies, shooting, movement hand to hand combat all in a easy to use hard to master D8 gaming system.

Objective of the game: play one of 5 different missions featuring two or more armies pitched against each other. The 5 different missions have various objectives, a few examples being to escape from the map or to hold out against the zombie horde player for a certain number of turns. An average game starts out at around 200 - 250 points per side. The game is scalable allowing for team battles and much higher point valued games.

Items required to play: Table top, terrain, miniatures, rule book, a few D8 dice and 2+ players.  No new miniatures needed!

In Lords of the Sand, each player takes on the role of one of four different gods: Anubis, Thoth, Sobek, and Khnum. Each god has its own unique agenda and special abilities.

Throughout the course of the game, players use Units, complete their court, use special abilities, and build Obelisks. With a combo-driven card system, as cards are played, additional actions may spawn multiple abilities, but a limited action system keeps downtime to a minimum.

Each player scores points for the Obelisks he has built, his leader, and the cards in his Court.

Lords of the Sand offers multiple scoring plaths, unique card play, and various special abilities with a playing time of about thirty minutes, plus ten minutes for each player above two.

In Crimson Sun, players take on the role of leaders of Upper and Lower Egypt. Playing units of four unique gods (Kuk, Babi, Horus, and Sekmet), players build their forces and attempt to assert influence over four key structures. Players may choose to either draft decks at the beginning of the game, or use a single deck.

During the game, players use the Unit cards in a variety of ways to increase his overall strength, to use special abilities, or to secretly try to gain control over the structures. The core mechanisms of this game are pulled from a previous Small Box Games' release — Bhazum — but mechanical and thematic changes have led to a game that should be a fresh experience for players already familiar with that game.

Players in Raben Stapeln mit Schnabelgrün want to do just what the title tells them: Stack the ravens with the green beaks. You don't just have ravens, however, but also discs and four- and five-pointed stars. Either working together or against one another depending on the rule set you use, players use the color die to keep the game moving and the pieces stacking...

Hoplomachus [ha-ploh-ma-kuss], or "Hoplo" for short, is a hex-based tactical board game set inside a gladiatorial arena. One to four players, each representing an ancient city/civilization thought to have been lost to the world, are now on Rome's doorstep vying for a place in the prestigious arena games.

Players have a very clear objective: Eliminate the opponent's champion. Champions start the game in the arena but are inactive and defenseless. They will not fight until the crowd is behind them. Each player takes turns drawing, playing and moving gladiators from their "hand" and assisting them with tactic chips.

Based on opponent's moves and play style, each person will need to adjust their strategies. Defend your champ, take over and hold the crowd favor areas, assault opponent's champion, cover deployment zones, eliminate tactictions to reduce enemy options, fight beasts to gain crowd favor, the list of tactical possibilities is endless.

Hoplomachus combines combat, tactics, chip (units) management, level progression (though crowd favor system), team play, player vs board, and so much more.

Beast & Master: These 10 chips include an all new Gladiator and Beast for each of the 5 Cities that Hoplomachus has to offer. These will be the start of what will be the 'customizable' portion of the game. Playing with the Beasts and Beastmasters option will be determined before each battle begins!

Carpe Venatus!

description:
Story: By treading the path through this labyrinth, the players enter the undiscovered world of their own being...

Essence: Im Labyrinth des Lebens (Essence: In The Labyrinth Of Life) transforms the card game into a board game and provides it with an atmospheric game board, which replaces the pathway cards of the base game.

By the use of two dice, the players still have the possibility to choose between two options. Newly added are actions for the centre of the labyrinth and for the encounter of meeples.

The player who reaches the centre decides if the game is continued or ends immediately. In this case, essence points are counted and the winner is determined.

The gameboard is made of tarpaulin which makes it rollable, waterproof and very durable.

Hexehop is a Halma variant that was designed to allow for players with impaired vision.  Its board is a hexagonal grid of points, seven to a side, but with two points from each corner removed.  Also, there are no lines connecting the inner hexagon of five points to a side with the outer portion of the board, resulting in six separate starting areas of nine points each, outside the inner hexagon.  The lines and points are done in relief, and the pieces are made not only in colors but in shapes.

The game is played the same as Halma or Chinese Checkers, with up to six players taking turns moving their pieces one at a time to the opposite side of the board.  Pieces may move to one adjacent point or jump another piece to a vacant opposite point.  The first play to move all of his or pieces to their objective wins the game.

Hive is a strategic game for two players that is not restricted by a board and can be played anywhere on any flat surface. The base game of Hive is made up of twenty two pieces, eleven black and eleven white, resembling a variety of creatures each with a unique way of moving. Hive Carbon and Hive Pocket include the Mosquito and Ladybug expansions, for a total of 26 pieces.

With no setting up to do, the game begins when the first piece is placed down. As the subsequent pieces are placed this forms a pattern that becomes the playing surface. (The pieces themselves become the board.) Unlike other such games, the pieces are never eliminated and not all have to be played. The object of the game is to totally surround your opponent's queen, while at the same time trying to block your opponent from doing likewise to your queen. The first player to totally surround the opponent's queen wins the game.
family: Versions & Editions: Travel Versions of Non-Travel Games

description:
On a magnetic game board, players in Space Station take turns building cities, stations and spaceships; invading the stations owned by opponents, and expanding their cities and stations.

To set up, players place a predetermined number of their pieces on the game board, based on the number of players; pieces come in three sizes: cities (large), stations (medium) and spaceships (small). On a turn, you take one of the following actions:


    Build a city, station or spaceship — Take one of these tokens from your reserve and place it next to a friendly token of the same size or larger already on the board.



    Invade an opponent's city or station — If one of your pieces touches an opponent's, you can place one of your smaller tokens on top of it.



    Establish a spaceship base — If you have a token on top of an opponent's city or station, you can place one of your spaceships next to this invaded piece.



    Expand to a city or station — If you have two spaceships or a spaceship and station close to one another, you can place one of your stations or cities so that it touches both of these pieces. You can expand with pieces that have invaded opponents, and if you're set up right, you can both expand and invade at the same time!


Players keep placing pieces until everyone has passed in turn because no space remains on the game board. Players score two points for each uninvaded station of their color on the game board, then lose one point for each station not placed. Whoever has the highest score wins!

In 2050, the Mississippi River is toxic sewage draining directly from the interior of Moloch. The river and its vicinity hidden in poisonous fumes are known as the Belt of Death or seedbed of mutants. Warriors of the Mississippi, clad in coats and gas masks, diffuse the worst of the diseases and poisons. They are literally immune to everything. Admittedly, they rarely leave their beloved sewage, but when they move their hard asses, you can be sure that they will withstand the contaminations longer than you as they saw practically everything while growing up near that damned river!

Neuroshima Hex! Mississippi is the fifth army pack for Neuroshima Hex! This tough, defensive army's main advantage is the ability to massively poison the enemy HQ with venom as well as to thwart the enemy plans. The other advantages are noticeable resistance to attacks and ability to bypass the enemy's defense. The army's disadvantages are low number of Warriors, low mobility, low toughness, as well as a complete lack of armor.

The Myth: Muckers Captain Pack consists of two mucker captain miniatures identical to those used in the base Myth game. Muckers lead the Grubbers, allowing more of them to spawn from lairs while also being able to knock heroes prone. In addition, muckers are more difficult to bring down.

7 Wonders: Babel includes two modules for use with the 7 Wonders base game, and they can be used individually or together in any combination with other expansions.

In one half of 7 Wonders: Babel, players draft quarter-circle tiles at the start of the game prior to drafting anything else; each tile depicts a law that affects all players should it be put into play, e.g., all single resource cards provide an infinite number of resources each turn, or winners in military conflicts receive fewer points than normal.

During the game, players now have an additional option when discarding a card. Instead of gaining three coins, they place one of these tiles in the next open space on a circular display; the law on this tile remains in effect until the end of the game or until it's covered. (Should a fifth tile be placed, for example, it's placed on top of the first tile played.) At the end of the game, players receive points based on how many tiles they played.

In the second half of 7 Wonders: Babel, one of five age specific great project cards is randomly revealed at the start of each age, and a number of tokens are placed on it, based on the number of players. This card imposes a tax on players who want to play cards of a certain color. When a player pays this tax, he takes one of the tokens from this law card. At the end of the age, if all of the tokens have been removed, then players receive a bonus (which is depicted on the card) for each token they have; if tokens remain on the card, then each player without a token is penalized.

Just as the cost of cards increases in each age, the number of resources required to pay the tax also increases.

Grab a mallet and smack away on the lever at the base of the play set. With every smack, moles fly out of the molehill and onto the ground. Use the special mole-catching mallet to collect the moles. Be the first player to fill your mallet with moles to have your “mallet” mole reach all the way to the top wins. Includes 2 mallets, moles, and three different levels of play.

A set of 3 Kickstater exclusive cards that were available during the Fantastiqa: Enchanted Edition campaign.

It reads:
Each player must choose one card in her hand to feed to the Ravenous Raven. Release the chosen card from play.

You may store one card on top of this card.
The stored card may be later used (or discarded) as you choose. After the stored card is removed, discard the Beast of Burden.

Summary 

UFO Defense Protocol is a strategy boardgame for 2 to 7 players, which can be played as a team versus team game (humans versus aliens), or alternatively, as a full cooperative game (all the players against the game).

The game takes place in a situation of emergency.
The existence of aliens has been discovered, and they threaten to invade the planet.
The players either control a secret cell of humans or an imperial alien faction.
They fight each other to protect or conquer the planet.

The game features construction, research, squad combat and air battles.
All decisions and strategic actions are done in simultaneous turns. Ground and air battles still need to be resolved separately.

The game features 7 factions (4 humans, 3 aliens), with their own abilities and special units.



General gameplay 

Every turn, Alien factions adapt stronger warships, clone more soldiers, and prepare their mothership.
The objective of the alien team is to successfully complete a mothership and have it land on planet Earth.

To counter the alien invasion, human factions recruit soldiers, launch attack jets and develop secret weapons.
The objective of the human team is to build a spacecraft, and have it destroy the alien colony. They must also down any mothership that would come.

Other victory options are available.
Aliens can win the game if they terrorize enough cities to create such chaos that the human directors will be dismissed.
This happens immediately if the Terror Counter reaches 13.

And any team wins the game if at the end of 12 turns, no team has met victory conditions, and it has the most Prestige.


Game modes: Full Cooperative or Team vs Team 

The normal game can be played with any number of players from 2 to 7, and can have an asymetric number of players.

Players can choose to play in the Earth United mode, in full cooperation. 
Or they can play in the default Alien Invasion mode, with a team of aliens versus a team of humans.

Each player picks a faction at the beginning of the game.
Earth United is limited to the human factions (4 players).
In Alien Invasion, there are 4 human factions and 3 alien factions available.

Two players advance their pieces across a grid of spots, according to a single rule of movement... which depends upon the number of pieces in a straight or ricocheted line. Groups of pieces may move in the direction of their axis up to a number of spaces equal to the number of pieces in that group.

The object is to move your pieces off the opposite end of the board first.

The playing surface is a fabric bag; the playing pieces are miscellaneous objects - drop them in the bag, roll it up, and snap it shut for easy carrying or storage.

Objective: Gain most victory points by achieving valuable parts of a bird, acting by turns.

A beautiful bird (composed of paperboard parts with spots of dice/numbered from 2 to 12) is arranged first.

Active player throws the dice and may take a corresponding part if there are still any; another roll is allowed if first one was successful. Some inner parts may be taken only if all surrounding ones are gone.

Notes:
Likely published before WWII

The goal of the game is to get one's bug one time around the game board. The first to achive this, wins the game. But beware the maid Fifi! She prowls the game board and shots off the legs of the bugs. Also, whenever two bugs meet, the arriving bug bites one leg off the other bug. The Bugs can only move when they have all six legs attached. To attach a lost leg, they have to roll a 1 or 6. The game is played with 2 dice. The red one determines the movement (clockwise) of the bugs, while the blue one moves the maid counterclockwise.

The game was most likely published in the 1930s.

boardgame: Angry Birds: Star Wars – Jenga Bespin Battle Game
description:
This is another game based on the popular angry birds app.  This is a Target exclusive game that includes a Han Solo bird, Boba Fett pig, Stormtrooper pig, Millennium Falcon launcher, 8 full jenga blocks, and 3 half blocks.

This is designed for 1-2 players age 8 and up.

One player builds the structure and the other player launches his bird attempting to knock down the pigs and the entire structure.  This player keeps count of the number of shots taken.  Once the entire structure has been knocked down the roles are reversed.

The winner is the player that required the fewest number of shots to destroy the structure.

A game of questions, mainly based on Switzerland. Composed of 400 questions it covers a wide spectrum including, for example, the Vienna meeting of 1815, Facebook, or Swiss heroes.

Points are earned for every good answer and are showing your IQ. The player with the most IQ wins.

boardgame: Bruges: The City on the Zwin
description:
Bruges: The City on the Zwin, an expansion for Bruges, includes multiple modules that can be added to that base game individually or together.

First, Bruges: The City on the Zwin includes components for a fifth player to compete in the game, with a separate board showing the canal on which this player would build. To make the card deck larger — so that not all of the cards would be in play, as in the game as published — this expansion includes additional character cards that belong to a new guild. Some of the powers of these characters match those of the base game, but you'll also now have the ability to dig through the discard pile to pull characters back into play.

Second, the expansion includes a stack of stock cards, with each card having a special power that modifies existing actions. Only one card is in play at any time; it starts with five or six scroll counters on it, and each time this action is used, one counter is removed. Once the final counter is gone, someone places a new stock in play.

Third, whenever a 3 or 4 is rolled on a die at the start of a round, a ship of the same color is placed on the supplemental game board. As an extra action, a player can discard a worker of the appropriate color, reveal the matching ship, and take the action depicted, such as advancing on the influence track or reusing a one-time power on one of your character cards.

Finally, Bruges: The City on the Zwin includes statues of values 8 and 9 to give players more incentive to complete canals as well as a revised "Engraver" card to replace the one in the base game.

description:
Greetings, and welcome to the Slime Hunters Guild! It is our job to venture out into the wilds, track down those pesky creatures known as Slimes, and trap them in a manner that allows them to be brought back alive (alchemists, wizards, pet stores...surprisingly, there are many potential and wealthy buyers.)

Today, there appears to be an outbreak of them, which makes a great business opportunity for us! Take these "magic boxes" with you (the dice), and throw them in order to activate their trapping mechanisms. Should they result in the right symbols (roll the correct result), then they shall be baited correctly in order to attract and trap whatever Slime inhabits the area. Can you bring back a higher valued Bounty than the other hunters?

Contents:


     4    Dice for trapping Slimes
     32  Slime Cards 
    2     Instruction Cards
    2     Score Reference Cards
    1     Box


Gameplay Summary:

Each turn, 4 Slime Cards are drawn and placed in front of the active player. The active player must roll a certain number on the dice in order to trap one or more of them, depending on the colors drawn. If the player is unsatisfied with their dice roll, they may remove one of their dice in order to reroll all of the remaining dice and trap a higher number of slimes for the turn. Gameplay and turns continue this way until the Slime deck is completely gone through twice, then the players reference the Score Reference Card to figure up their scores and determine the winner.

Scoring:

Each Slime is worth 1 point on its own, but additional points bonuses are awarded when certain criteria are met, such as collecting 1 of each color or the most of a specific color.

Chanakya's Chakkravyuh is a unique learning tool in the form of a financial board game. Its main purpose is to help players from all walks of life (including common man, entrepreneurs, corporate professionals, students, educators, home makers, investors) attain financial literacy. Minimum age is 15 years and above.

Inspired by the 2000-year old wisdom of prominent Indian economist Chanakya, this game enlightens individuals on how to manage money and connect financial knowledge to resourceful opportunities, which are ever-present in today's changing world.

The player initially chooses a dream out of various dreams listed on the board. Each dream has a purchase price listed on it. In order to win the game, the player must purchase his/her pre-selected dream.

There are two parts in the game, the first is Chakkravyuh and second is Rajmarg. All players start inside the Chakkravyuh. In the first part of the game, a player may not have enough money to buy his/her dream. Chanakya's Chakkravyuh simulates real-life investment opportunities, various market scenarios, and unforeseen life situations to throw challenges that the participants have to tackle. By successfully navigating these challenges and deriving "passive income" (income where each rupee works to bring you even more rupees), participants eventually build enough income generating assets to move into the second part of the game, i.e. the Rajmarg. It is in the Rajmarg that the player can acquire their pre-selected "dream".

Even though there is no official time limit, the entire game normally lasts about 4 hours. The first player to purchase his/her dream wins the game, and that is officially the end of the game.

The experiential nature of a game-based environment makes the process of learning a lot more fun, and there is also a sense of competition among the participants.

Besides gaining financial knowledge and learning how to leverage passive income, participants will understand the real difference between an asset and a liability. Additionally, important lessons will be learned in basic accounting principles, debt financing, interpersonal communication, negotiations and joint deal-making.

No background in business or finance is required. Absolutely anyone can play this game.

boardgame: The Transvaal Ablaze: A Solitaire Game of the Boer Wars in South Africa
description:
This game is a semi-historical simulation of the Boer Rebellions in South Africa during the later part of the Victorian Era. The Game begins shortly after the Boers declare independence from Great Britain. The Boers have gathered Forces and are poised to launch assaults on several British Settlements in order to drive them out or force them into capitulation. It is the Player's task, as Commander of forces in South Africa, to squash these annoying rebellions and teach these uppity farmers a lesson.

OBJECTIVE: As the Commander of British Forces in South Africa, you must protect the British Settlements put down this rebellion by capturing their Capital of Pretoria before the Campaign Deck runs out.

GAME FEATURES: The game features a fun and simplistic method for resolving solitaire combat tactically, rules for Heroes that can make an impact on the outcome of battles, and lots of colorful game counters. It even includes rules for a potential Zulu uprising.

The Transvaal Ablaze can be found at http://www.berserkergames.net/.

boardgame: Aces of Valor: A Solitaire Game of World War I Aerial Combat
description:
ACES OF VALOR is a solitaire Game where the Player assumes the role of a Squadron Commander in charge of 10 aircraft on the Western Front during World War I.

Your Goal is to manage your Aircraft in order to Complete a Campaign, consisting of a series of Missions, and score enough Victory Points (VP) to assist the war efforts.

Campaigns may be Short (8 Missions), Moderate (12 Missions), or Long (16 Missions). The Player determines the length of Campaign to be played.

VPs are earned by destroying enemy aircraft and ground targets. You lose VPs by losing aircraft and failing to complete missions. The Player moves his/her Squadron Flight Counter on the Battlefield Map, however, combat is resolved easily off-map using an Inititiative Track. If you enjoy movies like "The Blue Max" or "Aces High", this game is for you.

Missions include Patrols, Trench Strafings, Recon, Photo Recon, Artillery Observation, Balloon Busting, and Bomber Escort. Rules provide means for Pilot improvement and the choice to fly for the Germans or British.

Most Games usually take 2-4 Hours to print out and assemble, depending on the number of counters, Cards and Mapsheets.

Optional Rules to play as the Lafayette Escadrille using Nieuport 11, Nieuport 17 and Spad VII Aircraft Counters.

Aces of Valor can be found at http://www.berserkergames.net

boardgame: The Khyber Ablaze: Uprisings and Punitive Expeditions on the North-West Frontier
description:
The Khyber Ablaze places the Player in command of the British Forces along the North-West Frontier and Afghanistan in the later part of the Victorian Era. You must use the forces available to you to put down a wave of uprisings, led by a fantatical Leader, that threaten to throw the British out of the region. If you liked movies like "Gunga Din", "The Brigand of Kandahar", "Flame over India" and "Lives of the Bengal Lancers", this game is for you.

OBJECTIVE: As the Commander of British Forces along the North-West Frontier, the Player must protect the British Settlements and defeat the Khan’s Forces by capturing three of their four Fortified Settlements of Samagan, Konduz, Herat and Kandahar before time runs out or the Khan’s Forces capture enough British Settlements to drive you Back to India.

GAME FEATURES: The game features a fun and simplistic method for resolving solitaire combat tactically, rules for Heroes that can make an impact on the outcome of battles, and lots of colorful game counters.

boardgame: The Sudan Ablaze: Rebellions and Uprisings in the Sudan
description:
THE SUDAN ABLAZE is semi-historical simulation of the Mahdi Uprisings in the Sudan during the later stage of the Victorian Era.  As the Commander of British Forces in the Egypt and the Sudan, you must protect the British Settlements and squash the swelling fanatical Sudanese forces.

OBJECTIVE: As the Commander of British Forces in the Egypt and the Sudan, the Player must use his forces to protect the British Settlements and squash the Forces of the Mahdi by capturing their Capital at El Obeid in South-Western Sudan before the Campaign Deck runs out.

GAME FEATURES: The game features a fun and simplistic method for resolving solitaire combat tactically, rules for Heroes that can make an impact on the outcome of battles, and lots of colorful game counters. If you enjoy movies like "The Four Feathers", "Khartoum" or "The Light that Failed", you will enjoy this game.

boardgame: The Wilderness Ablaze: A Solitaire Game of the French and Indian War
description:
The Wilderness Ablaze is a semi-historical simulation of the French and Indian War in the American Colonies during the Mid-1700s. Players assume the role of the Commander of Forces in the American Colonies in the turbulent times where both France and Britain are seeking to increase their nation's territory and power. Can you defeat the French by capturing Montreal and preventing them from capturing British settlements?

OBJECTIVE: As the Commander of British Forces in the American Colonies, you must protect the British Settlements and attempt to capture the Key French Settlements of Quebec, Montreal and Toronto before the Campaign Deck runs out.

GAME FEATURES: The game features a fun and simplistic method for resolving solitaire combat tactically, rules for Heroes that can make an impact on the outcome of battles, and lots of colorful game counters. If you enjoy movies like "Last of the Mohicans", "Pathfinder" or "The Northwest Passage", you will enjoy this game.

The Antares Galaxy System is a merging inter-planetary commerce system, however, not all is blissful and peaceful in the Antares System. One Planet, Xanthar, has grown particularly powerful under the oppressive leadership of a power-hungry, ruthless and brutal ruler, T’arNak!  Meanwhile, you and the other players are Free Merchant Traders, buying commodities from a Planet with a large supply and selling them to Planets in need. Now, it is getting a bit tricky and dangerous as T’arNak’s Star Legions are increasing their presence and enforcing their strict trade regulations. The citizens of the Antares System need brave, heroes like you to help them throw off  the yoke of  T’arNak’s oppression.

The goal of the Players, is to gain the most Star Credits (SC) before the end of the 20th Round. The Player with the most Credits is the winner. In addition, the Heroes will undertake various missions to thwart T’arNak’s operations. The game automatically ends when the first Hero successfully completes 10 of these “Special” missions.  If playing solo, the goal is to survive and gain 10,000 Credits or complete 10 Special Missions.

You can find Galactic Smugglers at the following URL:
http://www.berserkergames.net

boardgame: Heroes of Valdor: A Board Game of Fantasy Adventure
description:
HEROES OF VALDOR is a fantasy adventure board game where the players assume the roles of Heroic Characters in the savage world of Valdor, a place where three races (Human, Elf and Dwarf) have lived and fought for millennia with only a few scattered decades where peace was declared between the races. Now in the east, an invasion is occurring; an invasion by an evil and foul race of Orcs along with their minions of Goblins, Trolls, and a variety of terrifying creatures, led by their powerful and terrifying Sorcerer Overlord by the name of Valtigorn.

It is up to the Heroes to help unite the various races and tribes of Valdor in order to gather enough forces to defeat the Army of Valtigorn.

HEROES OF VALDOR, also, features the amazing Artwork of the extremely talented Mr. Devin Night.

HEROES OF VALDOR is a rich and encompassing Fantasy game that will emerge the Players into Magical and Fantasic World.
