These Army List specifically support the 6th Edition of the War Games Rules.

Dixit: Daydreams is an expansion for Dixit that contains 84 new cards for use with that game. In Dixit, each round one player takes on the role of Storyteller, choosing one card from his hand, then telling a story, singing a ditty, or otherwise doing something that in his opinion is associated with the played card. Each other player then chooses one card in her own hand and gives it to the Storyteller in secret. These cards are shuffled and revealed, then players vote on which card was played by the Storyteller. If either no one or everyone votes for the Storyteller, then he receives no points; if he received some votes but not all of the votes, he scores based on the number of votes received. Each player who submitted a correct vote or who received a vote on her card submission also scores. After a certain number of rounds, the player with the most points wins.

boardgame: Skylanders Swap Force: Battle of the Elements Game
description:
It's the exciting, head to head, Skylanders board game. Players roll their die trying to be the first player to match their Skylanders special element. But beware of Kaos, if he shows up that will cost you. Collect the most cards and win!

Players each have a side of 5 Skylanders on their force and a die with the elemental symbol of each of their 5 Skylanders and a wild symbol.  The one battle at a time, the two Skylanders across from each other do battle.  Both players roll their dice simultaneously, if their Skylander's symbol comes up, they win and collect the two Skylander cards.  A wild is considered a symbol, and the Kaos symbol is considered a loss.  Ties are re-rolled.  This is done for each Skylander.  The only exception is the Kaos Skylander, in which case you lose points if you collect them, so you hope they are taken by the other person instead.

The player with the the most points (Skylander cards minus Kaos cards) wins.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements from Traunstein, in Germany.

A Kickstarter exclusive promo card for Stak Bots.

It has a moderately high power value, and allows you to play a card on an opponent's stack when it comes into play.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements from the Golf association, located in Switzerland.

This special edition of Monopoly celebrates the growth of travel in the UK through the years.

Monopoly Overview
Players take the part of land owners, attempting to buy and then develop their land. Income is gained by other players visiting their properties and money is spent when they visit properties belonging to other players. When times get tough, players may have to mortgage their properties to raise cash for fines, taxes and other misfortunes.

Game play
On his turn, a player rolls two dice and moves that number of spaces around the board. If the player lands on an as-yet-unowned property, he has the opportunity to buy it and add it to his portfolio or allow the bank to auction it to the highest bidder. If a player owns all the spaces within a color group, he may then build houses and hotels on these spaces, generating even more income from opponents who land there. If he lands on a property owned by another player, he must pay that player rent according to the value of the land and any buildings on it. There are other places on the board which can not be bought, but instead require the player to draw a card and perform the action on the card, pay taxes, collect income, or even go to jail.

Goal
The goal of the game is to be the last player remaining with any money.

A Kickstarter exclusive promo card for Stak Bots.

It has a high power value, but must be chosen for any effects played.

In Babababa Burger, you are working in a busy burger shop.  In order to fill the orders, you'll need to collect the ingredients for making the perfect burger for each customer that comes into the shop.

There are four decks of ingredients: a red deck that includes beef patties and fried eggs, a yellow deck that includes chicken nuggets and french fries, a blue deck that includes pickles and cheese, and a green deck that includes tomatoes and onions.  All four decks also include buns.  You start the game with two food preparation areas and a card from each deck.  Three orders are turned up that can be filled.  A fourth order is turned up on top of the deck, this is a future order that will become available when one of the current orders is filled.  On your turn, you can play ingredients into your two food prep areas.  Then you place your worker token next to one of the four decks.  You draw two cards from that deck and place them under your token.  These cards can be taken when it is your turn again.  If someone also places their token next to the same deck, then you can take those cards at that time.  If at the end of your turn you have more than four cards in your hand, you have to trash ingredients until you are down to four cards.  Once cards are in the trash, you may also send your worker to get ingredients from the trash--with the added benefit that you get to choose which two cards from the trash you put under your token.  The game continues until one of the decks is exhausted.  Add up the total value of the orders each player has filled.  The player with the highest value wins.

Trio is a game for three, played an a grid of 52 triangular cells (in two alternating colors) arranged in the shape of an equilateral triangle, but with its corners cut off and replaced with a curved line connecting the outer edges of the board, forming its own cell.  Each of these additional cells is called a Fortress and belongs to a player.

Each player has three kinds of pieces in their own color: one General, four Officers, and seven Soldiers.  To begin the game, the General pieces are placed in the middle triangle of the first row adjoining that player's Fortress.  Filling out that row are the four Officers.  The next row inward has seven triangles and each are occupied by Soldiers.  These two rows are known as each player's Zone.

The object of the game is to move a General into an opponent's Fortress.  A cell may be occupied by only one piece at a time; players capture each other's pieces by landing on the same cell and removing the opponent's piece from the board.  Capture is not compulsory, and continuing moves after a capture is not permitted.  Movement of the pieces is through the sides of the triangular cells, not the points.

A Soldier moves to one adjacent cell in any direction except backwards towards his home Fortress.  An Officer moves across two adjacent cells, unless there is a piece he wishes to capture in one adjacent cell.  After capturing, he must remain in that cell until the next turn.  While in their own Zone, Officers may not move back towards their own Fortress.  Once out of their Zone, Officers may move in any direction.  Generals move across one or two adjacent cells in any combination of directions, so long as they do not return to the same cell on the same turn.

The first player to move his General into an opponent's Fortress wins the game.  Since it is possible for any piece to capture any other piece, it is possible that all three Generals could be captured and no player would win.

boardgame: The Raccoon is at the Candy Shoppe
description:
You are an international Spymaster for a global power during the Cold War. Uses your influence and espionage skills to sway the greatest spies in the world to your cause.

The Raccoon, the greatest spy the Cold War has ever knewn, is up for grabs. As a overseer for one of the great superpowers try and recruit The Raccoon, and his fellow super-spies, to your cause. By peddling influence, sending agents on strategic missions and carefully timing your secret plots you can sway these critical soldiers in the shadows and declare victory without dropping a single bomb.

The Raccoon is at the Candy Shoppe challenges 3-6 players to deduce who is friend and who is foe. Use your table position to send your suspected enemies’ agents into danger or to control the outcome of an assignment. Will your carefully laid traps eliminate a dangerous spy or will your most trusted resource double cross you?

Each turn you will send a spy out to a hotspot in the Cold War. When they arrive all spymasters will secretly spend tokens to influence the spy toward their goal or to twist the outcome of the mission. After all available spies have been placed players take turns revealing the tokens at each location and choosing how the mission will resolve. If your ideology has the most spies on your side at the end of the game, you win the Cold War!

boardgame: Sawbones: A Card Game of Misguided Medicine
description:
The history of medicine is gruesome, painful and sad, but as Steve Allen taught us “Tragedy plus time equals comedy.” So I guess what we’re saying is that using a red hot poker to scorch your hemorrhoids is a pretty hilarious concept once we have 700 years distance.

In Sawbones, inspired by the amazing medical history podcast from MaximumFun.org, you are bumbling “Doctors” trying to save a dying patient. Since your knowledge of medice is often limited to “Put Leeches all Over Her” or “Transplant These Goat Testicles Into Him” your Patient is unlikely to survive. Lucky for you you have other Doctors to share the Blame with.

Pick the least worst medical practice you can and try and shift the blame to your fellow physicians. Be quick though as only the Doctor with the least Blame will win when the Patient croaks.

Stalagbite! is a game for 2-4 players where you take on the roles of dwarves heading into the deep caverns beneath the Lost Mountains in search of Gold and Glory. Dwarves hammer at the rock formations looking for gold, gems and crystals, but they risk uncovering hazards such as rock falls, deep pools, jagged pits and the primordial horror known as the Stalagbite!

While each player is in theory out to win the game by collecting the most gold, player’s dwarves also earn Hero Points by killing monsters and rescuing comrades. The player with the most points, in both treasure and glory, at the end of the game is the winner.

Glory & Riches is a strategic game of resource management and area control for 2-5 players. Each person is a fief lord vying for the kingdom's throne. To gain the throne, a player needs to control three fief Capital cities.

During the game, you produce resources, construct buildings, train peasants into advanced units, and expand your territory. The interesting part of the game involves dueling expansion mechanisms. Players pursue economic expansion and/or military conquest on their path to the throne, which means that you can either bribe the loyalty of neighboring cities or trample them under the boots of your armies. The game typically takes 30 minutes per player.

boardgame: The Game of Circination or Swinging Round the Circle
description:
Circination is a puzzle and strategy game, resembling Chinese Checkers, but played with round pegs.

Circination is a game for two players. There are 36 white pieces and 36 black pieces. The object is to get these pieces around the circles on the board and off the board when home.

Typical spin and move children's game. Each player selects a city and puts his piece upon the Railroad Station. Spin the indicator in turn, moving forward as many spaces as spin. The player who first reach the Station at Boston by exact spin wins the game.

Hello Kitty Äppelspelet

Ta dig upp för stegen och plocka äpplen. Den med flest maskfria äpplen vinner.

Hello Kitty The Apple Game

In this game you help Kitty and her friends to gather apples. Roll the dice, climb the tree, and gather apples. But beware! Some apples have worms and should be avoided.

Kastellet is a game for two, played on a hexagonal board comprised of sixty-one smaller hexagons, five to each side.  The central hex is referred to as the Point, and may not be played upon by either participant.  There is a unique neutral piece called the “Roy” which is used to mark the Point.  Each player has thirteen pieces in their own color, as follows: five Flag pieces, four Maces, three Lances, and one Bar.  Only one piece may be on a hex at a time; landing on an occupied hex is a capture, and the opponent's piece is removed from the game.  The object of the game is to continually “cover” the Point by always having at least one piece ready to move onto it.  Should a player lose his pieces covering the Point and not be able to move another piece into covering position on the next turn, he loses the game.

The game begins with the players at opposite ends of the board, with a five hexagon side closest to them.  Their five Flags are placed on those closest five hexes, the player's back line.  Flags only move forward to one adjacent hex, toward the opponent's end of the board.  Should a Flag reach the center hex of the opponent's back line, on the next turn (if it has not since been captured) it can be exchanged for that player's Bar piece, which is held in reserve at the start of the game.  The Bar can be placed anywhere on the board, but will remain there for the rest of the game, unable to be moved or captured by either player.  No further moves are allowed for that turn.

Maces begin the game two to a side on both ends of the players' Flags, extending around the rim of the board.  Maces may move to any adjacent hex, in any direction.

Lances begin in the central three hexes of the third row of hexes inward from the players' back lines.  The movement of a Lance is through the points and lines of the adjacent hexes to one of up to six hexes just beyond.  The game's hexes may have three alternating colors so that the color-bound moves of the Lances are obvious.  Because of its position on the board and its style of movement, the central of the three Lances begins the game covering the Roy.

Traders, Raiders & Runners is a strategic board game of competitive cargo route management.  Players take turns placing cargo-carrying Merchants and armed Runners to complete cargo routes and earn gold.  Raiders, or pirates, can be used to intercept and steal from Traders.

Each turn the cargo being shipped along each route will change randomly, altering the rules for placement and rewards for completed routes slightly.

The first player to accrue 20 gold wins.

Players must exhibit their mastery of the five elements in WuXing by trying to match the master in just the right way.

Each player starts with a hand of cards, with each card showing a hand; each finger of the hand shows one of the five elements on the tip. At the start of a round, someone flips over a card to represent the master's power. Each player then searches her hand for a card that matches the master's card on exactly three fingers — that is, elements on exactly two fingers have been swapped. If only one player finds such a card, she scores both her card and the master's; if more than one player has such a card, then you need to find an element on another player's card that's on the same finger on your card, with whoever does this first claiming all of the cards played.

The game ends when someone runs out of cards in hand, then whoever has collected the most cards wins.

Kung Fu Fighting is a card game that simulates an all-out brawl between 2-6 kung fu masters! Players are dealt a hand of cards, which include attacks, attack enhancements, weapons, blocks, chi restoration (healing) and fighting stances. They take turns playing cards from their hands to attack other players with stylish moves like the dreaded "Running up the Wall-Spinning-Flipping-Kick". A defending player can answer back with a block card, or counter attack with certain powerful cards. If an attack gets through, the defender loses chi (health). When a player runs out of chi, he is knocked out, and the battle continues until only one Kung Fu master — the True Master  — remains standing.

This edition of Kung Fu Fighting combines elements of the original Kung Fu Fighting (2004) and its expansion More Kung Fu Fighting (2008) with new game mechanisms.

This game consists of two sub-games. In the first game players take the role of journalists and try to get to the sat-van as quick as possible in order to upload the three pictures they have to capture in this game. The pictures are all Düsseldorf-buildings or sites and have a UFO sighting on them.

This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game, journalists try to get the cover-story before the other players can by running up the stairs of the publisher trying to beat the others to his office.

This game consists of two sub-games. In the first game players take the role of journalists and try to get to the sat-van as quick as possible in order to upload the three pictures they have to capture in this game. The pictures are all Dortmund-buildings or sites and have a UFO sighting on them.

This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game, journalists try to get the cover-story before the other players can by running up the stairs of the publisher trying to beat the others to his office.

This game consists of two sub-games. This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game, journalists try to get the cover-story before the other players can by running up the stairs of the publisher trying to beat the others to his office.

boardgame: Heroes of Argos: A Game of Heroic Adventure in Ancient Greece
description:
Each player represents a Greek Hero in the Heroic Age of Ancient Greece. Heroes attempt to complete great quests and gain fame, treasures and glory. The Hero that has accumulated the most Glory Points (GP) at the end of the Game Turns is declared the most renowned Hero of Greece!

As a Hero, you must gather followers, equip yourselves with weapons and armor, obtain a ship and venture throughout the mediterranean to complete Quests, slay monsters, and gain Glory to last through the ages.

Heroes of Argos features colorful and graphic counters of Heroes, Champions, Monsters, Ships, Weapons, Armor and a variety of encounters.

If you enjoy Greek Mythology, The Iliad, The Odyssey, or Jason and the Argonauts, you will enjoy this game.

boardgame: Operation Nam: A Solitaire Game of Tactical Combat in Vietnam
description:
OPERATION NAM is a solitaire game of Tactical Combat in Vietnam. The player assumes the role of a commander of combined forces in Vietnam. As commander, you are responsible for securing your Area of Operations (AOR) by successfully conducting various military operations in order to locate and destroy the enemy. Although there are many units available to you, they may often times be limited to you by political, logistical or geographical obstacles.

Game features Units with colorful graphics that include SEALS, Green Berets, PBRs, Hueys, Cobra Gunships, Leaders, Infantry Squads, Tanks, AFVs, Mortars, Artillery and Airstrikes.

Mission Actions and Combat are resolved on a tactical level.
If you are a Vietnam War enthusiast, you will enjoy this game!

Operation Nam can be found at www.berserkergames.net.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements from FC Schalke 04, a soccer club in Gelsenkirchen, Germany.

boardgame: Dogs of War: A Solitaire Game of Modern Mercenary Actions and Adventure
description:
Dogs of War is a game of Modern Mercenary Actions and Adventure. The player assumes the role of a Commander of a special group of Mercenaries available for hire by corporations and governments to complete hazardous missions. As the Commander, you must accept dangerous missions and assemble your Mercenaries in order to successfully resolve the missions.

GAME OBJECTIVE
The objective of Dogs of War is to successfully complete a number of missions in order to gain the highest profit, as determined by Resource Points (RP), for your outfit. The player can choose to play a short game (4 Missions), an average game (6 Missions) or a long game (8 Missions). Once all Missions have been resolved (successfully or not) the Player totals his RP earned and compares this with the Commander’s Profit Table to determine the level of success.

Game features Units with stunning graphics that include MERC Teams, Militia, Government Troops, PBRs, Hueys, MI-17s, Alouette Helicopters, Hind Gunships, Leaders, Tanks, AFVs, Mortars, Artillery and Airstrikes.

Mission Actions and Combat are resolved on a tactical level.

The battle of Vimeiro on August 21st, 1808 opposed Wellesley's British army to Junot's French army.

The British victory at Vimeiro made the French position in Portugal untenable. The Convention of Sintra was signed on 30 August and the French were transported back to France, with full honors.

Portugal would be free from the French until March, 1809.


La Bataille de Vimiero is not an official Clash of Arms game although it is produced with their approval.
family: Series: La Bataille (Marshal Enterprises / Clash of Arms Games)

description:
Trivia game about the 1980s. Has 500 different questions in 5 categories and is small enough to carry with you when traveling.

Players are dealt a handful of category cards, and on their turn they may play one of them and try to answer a question from that category. If they answer correctly, they may discard that card; otherwise, it's returned to their hand. The first one to get rid of all his cards is the winner.

In this variation to Monopoly, young players move around a board based on pets.

From the publisher:

Pet-opoly is like a trip through the pet store of your dreams! While you play, flip over the deeds and learn fun facts about pets and how to care for them.
publishers: Late for the Sky Production Company

description:
A word game where the players try to come up with words from a given category before the time runs out.

First, four random letter tiles are chosen and turned face-up (eg. S, K, W, N). Then, a category card is drawn from the deck and a die is thrown to determine the category (eg. "at the playground"). Players now turn the hourglass and write down all the words they can come up with that contain the chosen letters and match the category (eg. "sand", "swing", etc). When the time runs out, they score one point for each chosen letter they managed to use in their words; for example, "swing" would give 3 points for using S, W and N.

Contents: 29 letter tiles, 50 category cards, hourglass, pyramid-shaped die, scoring sheets, rule book.

Part of the Oppi&Ilo playful learning series by Sanoma Pro Oy.

A Monopoly edition for the very young ones.

From the publisher:
Take a trip to see the animals without even getting in the car. Collect footprints and trade them in for animals. Soon you'll find yourself a zookeeper in charge of lions, zebras, rhinos, polar bears and other fascinating wild animals.
publishers: Late for the Sky Production Company

description:
A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements from The National Gallery in the United Kingdom.

From the publisher:
The National Gallery houses one of the finest collections of European paintings in the world.
Explore the gallery by starting with Thirteenth-Century paintings, journeying by way of Renaissance masterpieces by Leonardo and Raphael, and finishing with Post-Impressionist works Van Gogh and Rousseau.

boardgame: Adventure Time Card Wars: For The Glory! Booster Pack
description:
Adventure Time Card Wars: For The Glory! contains expansion cards for both Adventure Time Card Wars: Finn vs. Jake and Adventure Time Card Wars: BMO vs. Lady Rainicorn, with the cards coming in randomly packaged booster packs of nine cards.

For The Glory! includes cards to improve every landscape type — including the Nicelands, which will be released in a later set — as well as cards that let you use your opponent's landscapes to your advantage and cards that promote the use of multiple landscape types in a single deck.

Players roll the dice and move around the board answering the questions about foods and cookery. 
The game board reminds a little bit Chutes and Ladders. If player must slide down he may answer a trivia question correctly instead. Player who finishes first is the winner.

There are 500 easy, 500 medium and 500 hard questions and 3 variants for each questions. Who answers correctly.

boardgame: A Fistful of Kung Fu: Hong Kong Movie Wargame Rules
description:
A Fistful of Kung Fu is a small scale miniature skirmish game based on A Song of Blades and Heroes and designed to recreate the action of Hong Kong action movies.

Each player has a "Protagonist" and 5-14 "Extras" - which could be selected from the Police, Ninjas, Martial Artists, Yakusa, Triads and Tongs, Supernatural Creatures, animals, or Cyborgs lists provided in the book - or pretty much anything they can imagine.

On a player's turn models are activated individually and dice for action points. For each success the model can take an action. For each failure the enemy protagonist has a chance to react. If two failures are rolled the player's turn ends and passes to the other player.

From the publishers website:

A Fistful of Kung Fu brings the hyper-kinetic, bullet-spraying, demon-slaying, kung-fu-fighting action of Hong Kong movies and Asian cinema to the wargames tabletop. In a modern world walking a precarious line between the advances of next-generation technology and the tradition and mysticism of ancient cultures, Kung Fu schools face off in no-holds-barred tournaments, corporations hire agents and spies to steal each other's secrets, overworked SWAT teams respond to gunfights between feuding Triad and Yakuza clans, and ancient artefacts are sought by hopping vampires, demon sorcerers and cyborgs alike. Combining the gunfights of John Woo’s Hard Boiled, the hand-to-hand combat of Enter the Dragon, the sheer mystical weirdness of Big Trouble in Little China, the wuxia action of Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon, and everything in-between, A Fistful of Kung Fu is a skirmish wargame unlike any other
family: Series: Song of Blades and Heroes

description: Re-live the great age of steam.  In Iron Horses: The Board Game, you are a captain of industry, seeking to expand your burgeoning railroad empire.  Players carefully build trains with engine, car, and caboose cards to earn the greatest profits.  Finished trains deliver goods to one of six regions in the country.  Making deliveries allows you to collect stock and manipulate prices.  Earn the most money by the end of the game to become the winner!

The completely irreverent, and somewhat crass, cardgame that pits Weasels and Foxes against steroid-enhanced Cocks.

The farm has become a dangerous place. Hens have become easy prey for the wild animals that roam the outskirts of the land. Each night the weasels and foxes sneak in to nab a nice dinner from the shelter of the chicken coop.

Lately, the farmers have not been as diligent as they should when it comes to the safety of their broods. The roosters have decided to stop their cock fighting and stand up against the attacking beasts. They band together and wait for an attack from the vile creatures, hoping their numbers can fend off the nightly attacks. All is not completely safe for the hens however, as the farmers still like the taste of poultry in their meals…

The Hen House is a fast paced card game for 2-8 players. Each player tries to defend their Hen House while trying to stop the other players from building up their brood.

description:
From the publisher:

"Top2Bottom is the first gay game of its kind - created for the gays, by the gays.

This game is just as outrageous & dirty as you and your friends... maybe even dirtier.

As simple to play as spotting a closeted republican, one player draws a pink card and reads it aloud, and each remaining player chooses one fierce white card to answer. To come out on top be the first player to pull out with six pink cards.

[No straights were harmed in the making of the game.]"

Gameplay is identical to the Apples to Apples / Cards Against Humanity style of party game. One player each round is a judge, and reads a question or fill-in-the-blank statement for which all other players must play a card to answer. The judge then picks the winning submission, typically the funniest, raunchiest or most outrageous.

Welcome to Monster Hero Academy! In this 30-minute game for 2-6 players, you and your friends play as academy teachers. You will select the best student to partake in a major test every season and gain reputation as they excel in it. Gain the most reputation by the end of the year, and you'll be crowned the Teacher of the Year!

Monster Hero Academy features a drafting mechanism in which each player chooses the best student to represent him. Players also guide their chosen students with numbered cards, which determine the score to earn the rewards in every test. But choose wisely as the numbered cards you select each turn will be removed from the game!

In times of greed pervades the lives of the Goddesses, the war of no longer inevitable. Five Goddess consisting of earth, water, fire, air, and ether agreed to compete for "sixth element". Elements which can not only increase strength but also can make the Goddess of Life.

In Sanskrit, Panchadevi means five goddesses, this is a card game that uses dice were also in play. So the factor of luck and strategy, interplay to be a winner.

Each player will play one of the five goddesses and fight each other to gain victory points (VP) by collecting card of elements. Each card has a different VP and each player was released to collect cards of any type of element. However, if the player manages to collect cards of the same elements as his goddess then amount of VP of that card mentioned multiplied by two.

The game will be more challenging because if the player collects more than three cards of the same elements, then VP of a number of card mentioned has aborted and each card will only be worth 1VP. At the end of the game, the whole of VP will be totaled and the player with the highest VP is declared the winner.

Peppermint is a pony which every child would like. A pony to love! But a pony needs to be taken care or regularly. It needs to be groomed, fed and its saddlery and cleaning utensils needs to be looked after.

The aim of the game is to collect the most peppermint medals.

In order to get those the player needs a set of 5 different tiles. The tiles are acquired by rolling the colour on a 6-sided dice with the horseshoe acting as a joker. A player can keep any colour that is rolled on his turn but whenever a colour comes up a second time he loses all tiles collected so far in this turn.
In addition players can trade coloured tiles among themselves in order to complete sets.

So children will learn about risk management.

boardgame: Legends of Andor: Journey to the North
description:
After many years of peace, the heroes of Andor have received a cry for help from Far Hadria, and they immediately undertake a perilous voyage to the North. On board an old sailing ship, they travel to foreign islands and struggle to protect the coasts from terrible sea monsters. What's more, time and time again they find themselves challenged by raging storms and the unleashed power of the sea itself.

Legends of Andor: Journey to the North, the first large expansion for Legends of Andor, includes a new map of the northern region of Andor, new legends, and more.

Potty Ponies is a roll and move dice throwing game where players will land on illustrated squares depicting country life. Most of the squares incorporate childlike humour coming directly from the designer's rural experiences.

Players are represented by ponies. They collect apples, carrots and sugar lumps as they progress around the board and encounter other animals. At the end of the game children have to use a score pad to add up their points.

Are you a hunter or prey? 
In this game, the roles can be reversed, and that you are a hunter or a boar makes no difference: You have to survive!

Abstract strategy game where players move disc-shaped pieces across a functional and original board.

The Huntsmen can move forward diagonally and vertically for one or two space. the first move of every huntsmen pieces is only in forward vertically for one or two spaces.
The Wild Boars can move forward diagonally and vertically for one, two, or three space. the first move of every Boars pieces is only in forward vertically for one, two, or three spaces.
The huntsmen can attack the Wild Boars every time, with legit moves. 
The Wild Boars can attack only if they made the TURNABOUT, and opponent pieces didn't, or if they didn't make the TURNABOUT, but the opponent pieces did, with legit moves.

When a piece reach a square occupied by an opponent piece, with legit moves and the right conditions, the attack is valid; if there is no right condition (Like TURNABOUT Boar and TURNABOUT Hunt) is not valid.

The piece attacked is removed from the board.

For Obtain the TURNABOUT a piece have to reach a start square of the opponent piece. Now, their moves are backwards.
For cancel the TURNABOUT, simply reach a start squre of your team.

wins the game who removes or blocks all opposing pieces.

boardgame: Super Dungeon Explore: Kaelly the Nether Strider
description:
Description from Publisher:

Smoke, shadow, and dark energy twist and lurk from the scarred lands known as the Nether Rifts. From the depths of this stygian abyss the Nether Elves appear. Renowned amongst her dark kin, Kaelly is an assassin without peer. Embrace the darkness with this unique mini-boss and should any oppose you—strike from the shadows!

THIS BOX CONTAINS:

- 1 fully assembled Kaelly the Nether Strider miniature.
- Kaelly the Nether Strider mini-boss game card.
- Unique "Assassin's Crossbow" treasure card.

Objective: Be the first reaching space 130
 - moving by turns -
Simple 'Roll & Move' game. Ending on a marked space follow instructions.

Objective: Move one of your 3 'Double'-figures back home - or eliminate all opponent's 'Doubles'
 - moving by turns/ forward or backwards/starting positions white & black -

Abstract strategy game where players move disc-shaped pieces across an 12 by 12 cross-hatched ("checker") board.

Pieces only move diagonally, and only one or two  space(s) at a time.  
If a player can move one of his/her pieces so that it jumps over adjacent pieces of their opponent (only foreward, laterally or backwards - NOT diagonally) into an empty space, that player captures the opponent's discs. Jumping moves must be taken when possible.
If one of your discs reaches opponent's home base it mutates to a 'Double' (max. 3 x, after that opponent's home base is blocked). The movement range of a 'Double' is limited down to one space.

Note:
Back of the board: 'Nine Men Morris'

Cards have three pairs of dice printed on them. You roll the dice and place a chip on the matching spot on a card. When a card has all three of your chips you call "Mine" and take that card. Rolling doubles lets you remove an opponent's chip or add a chip to a card, if it's not frozen. On your turn you may freeze a card by placing your pawn on it. No other player may play on that card.

The first person to collect the designated number of cards (3-5 depending on the number of players) wins the game. With a little strategy, offense, defense, and some luck you can make the game yours.

Originally published by the Society of Ancients in 1969, this set of rules was a late development of the simple Bath/Featherstone system that held sway before the days of Wargames Research Group. It is widely considered to have been the ultimate in 'fun' rules of the time.  The final publication was in booklet form in 1984.

These miniatures rules are semi-skirmish level rules allowing for combats in open field, around fortifications (sieges), or on shipboard (rivercraft or boats).

Players create their own scenarios and victory conditions.  Gameplay follows the typical 1960s style of simultaneous action via written orders and combat resolution via die rolls and modifiers familiar to anyone who has played early Donald Featherstone or Tony Bath ancient and medieval rules.

description:
Game description from the publisher:

Today is the Festival of Aether, and your chance to prove superior magical skills and wit against your peers. Summon raw Aether from the Aether Realm and trade shrewdly with the other magi to collect a variety of Runes and Magical Elements. Use your collection to cast the best combination of Spells before the Realm closes and claim the title of Master Mage!

Aether Magic provides an engaging and exciting experience for any Mage up to the challenge!

Trilos is a game system featuring Triangular Dominoes (twenty-four Trilos and one Joker,) two playing boards, and two tile holders that include clips for tracking scores.  Each Trilo has a number of pips along each side, one through nine, and has a white face on one side, black on the other.  The spots on each side are the same, so that on one side the numbers run clockwise, the other anti-clockwise, which is indicated by an extra mark.

The rules describe these games for play with Trilos:

Dom Trilo – a game of drawing Trilos and matching them on a table, in an attempt to rid one's hand of any remaining tiles

Stones and Windows – two players match randomly drawn Trilos around the blank edges of the small board (a triangular grid of 10 triangles the same size as the Trilos.)  The goal is to be the first to use all one's Trilos or, if reaching an unplayable position, to score the fewest points on remaining Trilos.

Rummy Trilo – three players draw seven Trilos and privately try to match a pattern drawn in the rulebook, taking turns swapping out pieces until someone completes the pattern.

Crazy Pavement – a more complicated version of Stones and Windows, with players trying to match pieces around the numbered sides of the small board, tracking several different ways to score points.  When the game becomes unplayable, the score determines the winner.

Khuti – two players lay Trilos on the larger board (which features a grid of twenty-two triangles,) attempting to fill the board while outscoring each other.

Khefren – two players engage in a slightly less difficult version of the above game, Khuti.

Two Patience Games – there are two solitaire games included in the rules, Pyramid Patience and Trilex Patience.

Troulette – a game of chance utilizing a scoring board drawn in the rules and the Trilos pieces.

Druids is played on a six-sided board divided into 88 triangular cells.  There are three long sides of the board, equivalent to six of the cell's sides in length, and three short sides, two cells in length.  The overall shape is of a triangle with it's points cut off, and the game's pieces (Stones) share this shape.  Half of the board's cells are recessed, and divided into three color groups: black for a triangular Field in the center of the board, and red, yellow, and green for each of the Camps in the corners of the board.  In each of the recessed Camp cells are a number of dots in one of these quantities: one, three, six, ten, and fifteen, with the smaller quantities being closer to the center of the board.

Each player starts play with nine Stones of the same color as his Camp, placed in all the cells of his Camp.  The game is played only in the recessed cells of the board.  Players take turns moving their Stones one at a time in an attempt to move all the stones of their color into one or both of their opponents' Camps.  Stones may move to any one vacant adjacent cell (there are up to six adjacent recessed areas) per turn, so long as they do not re-enter their own Camp.  Stones may jump over one opponent's piece to an empty adjacent cell on the opposite side; stones are not removed from the board.  Jumps over friendly Stones are not allowed.

The rules allow for final scores to be tallied based on the number of dots covered by each player in their opponent's Camps, and for alternate winning conditions.

An Edwardian board game with graphics based on Felixstowe's course and its famous Morrello Tower. Printed rules for the game pasted down to inside box top. Invented by Sir Frederick Frankland, Bart.

Typical spin and move game. The first player, who cross the road, wins the game.

Rod Soccer is a relatively inexpensive reimplementation of the dome-style hockey games found in many arcade halls. The game combines many aspects and game play of Rod Hockey and Foosball.

Using steel rods, plastic or metal soccer players slide along defined track segments. There are five players and one goalie per side, just like in its Rod Hockey predecessor. Players simultaneously turn and move their five metal rods to pass and shoot the ball. Games can be played using a timer or to a set number of points.

Various field and ball designs have been used over the years, attempting to capture the realistic feel of soccer, while reducing bounce and the ball being ejected from the game.

Skilled players can learn how to alter their shots between "ground" balls and volley shots.

The game is light enough to be played on any elevated surface (such as a dining table or coffee table). It remains as light as the Rod Hockey predecessor.

This game relies on placing money on a small board in accordance with dice thrown. The player places some money on the number that represents what the player throws. In the 1800 version, money placed equates to one cent.

If there is already a cent placed, the players takes that money and throws the dice again. He/she takes money as long as there is already money placed, and places money as soon as a number is thrown that is empty on the board.

If a player has no more money to place, the player may throw the dice once more. If the player does not receive money on that turn, the player is out.

The game continues until there is only one player left, who collects all the remaining money.

This game is related to Harlekin och hans sju korvar and Der Deutsche Hanswurst, though the game intent here is a game for money and placement of coins (the others are roll and move games)

Objective: Be the first moving all of your ten meeples into target positions (two coloured crosses)
 - moving by turns -
Each player chooses a colour placing his/her 10 meeples into starting positions (colour matching corner).

During a turn each player moves one own meeple (with a knight's move in Chess). Spaces with other meeples can't be entered, also opponent's target positions block. There are four central positions which must be cleaned during next turn; other wise your meeple is sent back to starting position.

DWARVES OF IRON PEAK is a 2-4 player board game that pits a troop of brave Dwarves against the Vile creatures that live in the lands around them. The Dwarf player must complete quests, while the Vile player must stop the Dwarves or destroy them entirely!

Über Epic is a social strategy game for 2-12 players that play on 2-4 teams. Players control characters to achieve Epic Goals, attack opponents' bases to capture their flag, and defend their own flag. Use walls to build your defenses to slow opponents from capturing your flag. Artifact cards — featuring single-use items or immediate effects that either hurt or help you — are located around the neutral zone, which is a circular board.

Teams gain Über Points (UPs) by scoring hits on opponents via ranged or melee attacks, achieving Epic Goals, or capturing an opponent's flag, and the team with the most UPs wins.

PRIME TIME: The Ratings Game is a card game that puts you in charge of a major television network.

In PRIME TIME: The Ratings Game you are the head of a television network. It is up to you to create shows that will attract the highest ratings and beat out your rivals. At the start of the game your schedule is a blank slate. You will have to program shows for Monday to Friday night. You do this by programming (playing) cards onto the table. These cards represent the shows, actors, commercial breaks and more. At the end of the game, whoever has the highest rated shows on each night will “win” that night. Each night is worth points. The player with the most points wins the game.

Cast the actors, come up with special twists and don’t forget to program some commercials to pay for it all. What shows you create and when you program them are all up to you. Will you fight for the coveted Thursday night-time slot with a prestigious big budget show or try to tie up Monday and Tuesday nights with cheap programming? It’s up to you to take your network to the top of the charts and be the leader of Prime Time.

description:
Bloemeneiland - the Island of Flowers - is a game for 2 or 3 players who are flower buyers for the King. Players must buy flowers from the local merchants and growers and send them by ship back to the Palace to gain favor at court. The player with the most favor after six rounds is the winner.

Playing the game:
Beginning with the round’s Starting Player, players take clockwise turns doing 2 actions in order.

Action 1: Players must move the Island Marker on the board in a clockwise direction from one Townsperson space to another (or to the Dock). They may move any number of spaces but only the first space moved is free. Each additional space costs 1 gold. If the marker stops in front of a Townsperson that player will receive a benefit if they choose. If the player stops at the Dock they
will cause all players to deliver flowers to the Ship.

Action 2: After the player visits a Townsperson they must move their numbered Player Marker to a Location which matches the Townsperson’s colored border. Locations occupied by another player may not be visited. You may then use the Location’s Special Action. If the Island Marker is at the Dock, no Locations may be used.

If the Island Marker is at the Dock, all players may now choose to deliver flowers to the Ship and gain Favor Points. Beginning with the round’s Starting Player and then continuing clockwise, each player may deliver one type of flower to the ship, but never more than the Palace currently demands as valued by the dice in play. After each delivery, adjust the corresponding die down by the number of flowers delivered. Anyone who delivers flowers receives Favor Points equal to what is currently shown on the Favor Point track above the Ship.

Game End: The game ends after the sixth Ship has sailed. In additional to the Favor Points earned during the game, each player receives an additional 1 Favor Point for every 3 flowers of each type they have in their possession. Gold is not worth any Favor Points. Whoever has collected the most Favor Points is the winner.

This game is an express version inspired by Santiago de Cuba by Michael Rieneck.

description:
The game is a turn based strategy game with economical macro game phase which includes:
 - Politics
- Trade
- Tech development 
- Building

and Micro game phase with tactical squad based combat which includes:
- Different soldier classes with skills trees
- Custom vehicle & troops loadout 
- Deep damage system for vehicles and troops 
- Meaningful troops moral and mental statuses
- Weather conditions which influence on stats of vehicles and troops
- Variety of mission types (Vip protection, convoy raids, hostage rescue, search and destroy etc .)
- Permadeath of soldiers

So here is a few words about game setting it's about post world war three, not very distant future with alternative history. World isn't in ruins (it's not a full post apocalypse but pretty messed up) but in a very bad state. Some terytories are in better shape, a rich ones of course live in a good cities with clean air water and ecologically clean food, poor people aren't so lucky they have to deal with desease, poor salaries raiders etc. Political map changed dramaticly some contries disappeared a new one emerged. The name of the game has a relation to the actual document of WWII between Germany and USSR the original text in ussr was kept in the special folder under number 34. The realtion is spiritual of course, it can give the mood of the uncertanty there is peace for now but for how long and at the same time war is continuing but on the different fronts in trade in politics and in actual "undercarpet" secret  combat by mercenary pmc.

A few words about gameplay:

Players control a group of mercenaries,who  is trying to survive in the world.After choosing a spawn region  players start roam the world map in turn base manner, trading, finding themselves in different random events,building their base and of course taking missions . Countries autonomously  act with each other,influencing the market  and  political situation(some political events can create a job oportunity with bigger challenge  and at the same time with bigger profits and reputation points). As for missions-There are many types of them they are played on a smaller maps with terrain also in the turn based manner(One of the things that we want to make is a very tactical game play where diceroll isn't a decisive thing but human skill is). All in all the game has more  of  rouge like feel  than a  classical wargame with particular objectives.  The process is the main fun part,but global goals also here(Player can get a citizenship of particular country and lead it to world  domination,take hold of the entire PMC market and be the one who pull the strings,also there are  some endings that are closely lore related).Also battle system can be used just for skirmishes.

(#@*^, it's fun!)

The official game of OverMen everywhere; "Bob"ball! offers the perfect combination of awesome and balls in a revolutionary, mind-altering format. This instant classic is a re-imagining of the longer-to-brew hit game you've heard only whispers about from another tier of consciousness.

"Bob"ball! is a game for two players. Try to save your mutant family and get them to the Pleasure Saucers before X-Day. Beware, there's only enough room for one more family. The other will be condemned to fry on Earth with the rest of the Normals.

So pull the wool over your own eyes, grab some dice, and praise "Bob" or kill him. You've never seen balls like these.

Use your "Bob"balls to bounce around along the grid to pick up family members and deliver them to the Pleasure Saucers. Each turn, roll three dice, assign the values to each "Bob"ball, and move. Make them hit your opponent's ball and transfer movement power to form chain reactions to keep them from scoring. Hitting another "Bob"ball stops yours and can transfer a family from one to another.

boardgame: The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – Trouble in Tharbad
description:
After finding their way through several encounters with hostile Dunlending warbands, a handful of Middle-earth’s greatest heroes finally arrive at the ancient, ruined city of Tharbad. There, they hope to locate the Dwarf named Nalir, who holds information that the White Wizard believes may prove valuable in the fight against the Dark Lord of Mordor. Finding the Dwarf proves to be a matter of very little difficult, but gaining the information he can provide proves to be a greater challenge...

In Trouble in Tharbad, the third Adventure Pack in "The Ring-maker" cycle for The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game, your heroes must help Nalir extricate himself from the unfortunate predicament into which he has placed himself. You must battle Orcs, slip through abandoned streets, and seek to elude the spies from Mordor who have also taken an interest in the Dwarf and his information. Throughout the scenario's course of events, you must remain vigilant in order to keep Nalir alive. Should he perish, so will your chances of returning his information to Saruman, and the White Wizard's efforts to counter the threat of Mordor will be diminished.

Along with its new scenario, Trouble in Tharbad introduces a new Silvan hero, along with three copies of each new player card. You'll find more support for the Silvan and Gondor traits, the Doomed X keyword, decks that keep their threat levels at twenty or below, and more!

description: Shuffle Dice plays like shuffleboard, with players trying to push objects to the end of the game board — without going over! — to land on the highest scoring regions. In this game, however, players roll dice instead of pushing discs, and the numbers rolled on the dice can multiply your score.

"You are sorcerous investors, speculating on the fates of various people and places. You might risk your sorcerous gravitas on the Forest and the Huntress, only to lose it all when a bad moon rises in the end. Or you might get a bargain on a Sailor and a Journey, striking it rich when your ship comes in on a favourable wave."

An arcane commodity speculation game of bidding and bluffing, where players attempt to purchase the most valuable cards with limited information about what their true worth will be.  This information takes the form of cards dealt at the beginning, known only to their owner.

The game is played in turns, with each turn an auction.  The active player chooses one card to put up for bidding, and the highest bidder pays the bank and takes the card.  At the end of the game, the hidden cards are revealed and determine the worth of each purchased card, which the bank pays out.  The richest player is the winner.

Players are scholars and scribes poring over (studying, scribbling, erasing, more scribbling) an ancient manuscript. By playing cards to the free-form play area and then placing cubes on them to record their contributions, they hope to have their work be recognized by the Queen at story time.

Scoring is similar to Acquire, where the person with the most cubes scores the normal value of a card, and the second-most gets half that value.  This scoring is triggered when someone plays a card with a low enough rank -- the more workers on a card, the more likely it'll get scored by a particular play.

The number of workers you place each turn is based on the number of matching suits touched by the card you play.  This leads to natural clusters of suits, shaped implicitly by the opinionated nature of the deck.

When the deck runs out, or a player runs out of workers, or a player reaches 100 points, the game ends with a final scoring round.  The player with the most points is the winner.

Patrol: WWII is a fast playing man-to-man skirmish system that recreates squad patrols in any theatre of the Second World War with as little as ten miniatures per side and a 4'x4' play area. Individual games last from 15 minutes to 1 hour allowing for multiple exciting games in an evening's play.

Patrol takes you beyond line ‘em up and shoot ‘em down miniature gaming. Opposing squads draw one of the 20 missions offering a large variety of tactical challenges that reflect historical tactics and weapon effects.

Stats and weapons for American, Commonwealth, Soviet, German, Italian, Japanese, and Partisans forces are included. Additional appendix expands the game into the Korean War with new weapons and missions.

Monstrous tanks, zooming jeeps with 30 cal blazing, flame throwers and mortars raining death, along with random battlefield events, create a chaotic and dangerous battlefield where a realistic morale system recreates the reactions of soldiers and their squad leader's attempts to motivate them.

A complete campaign lets you keep the same squad over multiple games (if you can keep them alive). Survivors gain experience and skills, scrounge for weapons and gear, and try to get some sleep before drawing replacements and ammunition for the next patrol.

The standalone expansion to Hero Brigade, the fast-paced heroic card game for 1-2 players that features elements of deck-building and positional card-battling. Take on the role of the Heroes or the Villains and prepare to throw down! Hero Brigade 2 introduces new Heroes and Villains and new rules for 3 and 4-player tag team battles in combination with the original game.

Each character card can be played in three different ways (deployed to your Party's Front Row, deployed to your Support Row, or used for a one-time game effect). Hero Brigade also offers unique twists on conventional deck-building mechanisms. Drawing extra cards and eliminating your starting cards can hurt you in the long term as you permanently lose cards from your deck each time you reshuffle and each time one of your characters is defeated. Running out of cards loses you the game!

Dicey Curves Deluxe Edition, available from TheGameCrafter.com, includes the base game (with new track cards) plus the DANGER! Expansion (with new on-track obstacle bits).

From the back of the box:
Dicey Curves is a party-style car racing game that is fun for the whole family! The track is built during the game from a deck of track cards, giving a different race every time. Roll dice and form combos to move your cars down the track, and use control chips to make adjustments when the dice aren't cooperating. Start your engines and get ready to roll!

Includes DANGER! Expansion. The DANGER! Expansion amps up the party game into a dash of take-that, dangers and damage.

A ziplock package containing two games for solo or partication play.

"The Elephant in the Room" by Graham Evans is a cooperative game in which the players take the role of velites (Roman light infantry) trying to kill or drive off a Carthaginian war elephant, controlled by the game system, before it can contact the Roman heavy infantry.

The game lasts about 30 minutes and requires four sets of dominoes.

"Greyhounds in the Slips" by Phil Steele is inspired by Shakespeare's description of the Siege of Harfleur in "Henry V".  A variant of the Society of Ancients' game Anno Domino, the aim for Henry is to get his men to storm the breach in the wall of Harfleur, while the French player must attempt to hold him off.

The game requires two sets of dominoes.

The Complete Collector's Edition of Viticulture quite simply is a box with distinctive art, with two [filled] compartments in it:  One for the Viticulture (2nd printing) complete box, and the other for the Tuscany complete box.  Thus, the Complete  Collector's Edition.

boardgame: Strike North: Japan Invades the USSR, 1941
description:
Strike North. Japan vs. the Soviet Union, 1941

Strike North presumes that Imperial Japan has decided to invade the Soviet Union in 1941, instead of going to war with the United States. Conceptually, a Japanese invasion of the Soviet Union would be in conjunction with the German attack on the USSR (Operation Barbarossa) on 22 June, 1941. The strategic possibilities of this alternative course are incredible; The Soviets will be under the pressure of fighting a two-front war. However, by following this strategy, the Japanese would be temporarily abandoning their planned invasions of the resource regions of the East Indies and beyond, leaving Japan less than a half-year of oil to sustain their war effort.
The map shows Northeast Asia as it was in 1941, with each hex on the map equaling roughly 37 miles (60 kilometers) from side to opposite side. Each game turn represents one month of operations. The units are brigades, divisions and corps.

Components: One 22" x 34" map & 228 counters

Paved With [my...] Intentions is a quick card-drafting, story-telling, party game. You will mix and match sentence fragments, balancing the story you want to tell about yourself against building a winning score—then read your creation aloud and hope a high score overshadows a potentially embarrassing or frightening self-portrait.

The official goal is to produce a lineup of seven cards with the (absolute) greatest value, but most of a player's effort goes into putting together a story they'll be satisfied reading aloud to the group—and in the event of a tie or someone scoring exactly zero, the best story wins (by player voting).

The story chunks on the cards contain elements of comedy, horror, working in an office, sexuality, religion, new-age mysticism, dreams, nightmares, and more, and are frequently only a sentence or a fragment of a sentence, so it's unusual for any single theme to dominate even a single player's hand. Some cards, or combinations of cards, are explicit in their depiction of sex and violence, which will either put players off the game or push them to create the wildest stories possible.

description:
WIN THE JEWELS, WIN THE GAME!

Battle with wizards, monsters, goblins and and everything in between on your way to the four corners. Travel thru fields, rivers & dark forests to collect the 4 jewels before your opponents beat you to it!

Who Starts This Mess? is a trick taking game.

The goal of the game is to end up with the most points at the end of the game.  The game lasts four rounds (hands) with seven tricks in each round.  Each trick is represented by a tile (which can be positive or negative).  All of the tiles are known and in the order they will come out.

A round consists of drafting your hand from all the available cards.  You can also get chips that count towards end game points in the drafting process.  After the draft, the hands are played out acquiring point tiles.  At the end of each round, player with the most tiles/tricks gets bonus points.  Also, you will be carrying over three cards into the next round.

End of the game, more points are gained by being in the top three in the number of tricks, highest total on your three remaining cards and the number of chips gained from drafting.

boardgame: Nova Suecia: The Last Letter Home
description:
Nova Suecia - the Board Game© is a board game about colonization, trade and survival in the New World. The players take on the role as governors of the Swedish colony Nova Suecia in the 17th century Delaware. They must cooperate to build a viable economy in the fragile colony since no player can control all parts on his or her own. The colonists need food, the colony needs money and the resources must be manufactured into tradable goods. The players must also, secretely, allocate common and own resources to the budget to prevent the colony from falling to internal and external threats. Each player must thus find a way to get the upper hand in this cooperative act of balance to emerge as a winner in the end. If they fail and the colony of Nova Suecia falls, all players lose!

Game mechanisms include

1. Player balance: The players grow by one district per turn and are thus always equally strong.
2. Game balance: The game has a delicate balance between wealth, food and production. If one area grows on behalf of the others, the game will fall and so will the players.
3. Bidding: All card drawings are followed by biddings where judgement rather than luck determines the outcome.
4. Production & Trade: The players build and develop the game together.
5. Taxation: The players must secretly choose how much to pay for common expenses, thus introducing free-rider and prisoners' dilemma challenges
6. Interdependence: The players must both cooperate and compete to survice.

WooWee is hands down the handsiest game around! This game of crazy hand gestures has all players playing simultaneously, attempting to get rid of all the cards in their deck. Each card has a saying [that needs to be said] and a gesture [that needs to be gested]. If your card doesn't match anyone else's, then your card goes to the center of the table. BUT, if your card does match another's, then you both must race to complete all the cards in the center of the table before you slap the BAM! card. Be the last to slap the BAM! and you get to add all the cards to your deck.

Challenge Sudoku and Kakuro Challenge in one box.

Sudoku is a game of placing numbers in a grid so that each number appears only once in each row or column. Challenge Sudoku offers three different ways to play: Standard, Challenge and Solitaire Sudoku.

In Kakuro Challenge, 2-4 players make rows on a board, on which they try to place their numbers in a way that the sum of those numbers is the same as a predetermined number.

For almost three hundred years no Coldrum set foot in Mossdown, a largely self-imposed exile recently ended by my acquisition of the ruins of our ancestral home. With the restoration of Coldrum Manor ongoing, there has never been a better time to document its history, and it seems apt that it should be a Coldrum to relate the tale.

Coldrum Manor, more commonly known as the derisory “Redcap Manor”, was built atop Mossdown Hill, a site long associated with spirits and faeries. Legend speaks of a series of standing stones that once stood near the hill, and Roman records of the area describe pagan rites involving human sacrifice. The strategic location of Mossdown Hill has long been recognised, and several strongholds have been constructed at this site over the last one thousand years.

'Redcap Manor' draws from the most traditional tales of haunting in the British Isles, in which it was faeries, not ghosts, that kept people awake at night. In 'Redcap Manor', each player represents one of four Edwardian investigators sent to the titular mansion, to gather as much evidence for the uncanny as they can over three nights. To this end, the protagonists will use the recently-harnessed power of electromagnetism against the Fae.

'Redcap Manor' is an auction/bluffing card game, divided into three turns representing the three nights of terror. Each turn, the players select a number of Haunt cards from their hand, to be secretly shuffled into the Haunt deck for the next night. Haunt cards are divided into three types: Fae, Presence, and Influence cards, each of which is countered by a different Ward card. Each player then places one of their three Ward cards face-down, and takes it in turns to uncover the top-most Haunt card. Eight cards are revealed per turn, representing eight hours before the dawn. After revealing a Haunt card, a player may choose to flee the Manor, but may not re-enter that night. The Haunt card remains face-up, and may be taken by subsequent players, together with any newly revealed cards. Alternatively, the player may take the Haunt card(s) - any Haunt cards countered by their Ward are taken as evidence (victory points); however, if the top-most card is not countered by their Ward, it is taken as a curse. Each Haunt card (24 unique cards, one for each hour of each night) has its own effect, influencing how the game is played. The player must determine whether taking a curse is worth the opportunity to acquire more evidence.

At the end of the third night, the player with the most evidence points is the victor.

boardgame: Smash Monster Rampage! Mega Monster Box
description:
The town is under attack again! Choose from 16 unique monsters to protect the town from, each with its own mechanics and cards.

The Mega Monster Box also introduces new unit types, weapon tokens for one-time attack bonuses, and variant Smash dice for a more challenging game.

boardgame: Conquest of Ethiopia: The Italian Invasion, 1935-1936 – A Panzer Grenadier Game
description:
From the Avalanche Press website:

Without the formality of a declaration of war, in October 1935 Italian armies stormed into Ethiopia from both north and south. Badly outgunned and outnumbered, the Imperial Ethiopian Army resisted for six months before its final collapse. Italian forces deployed tanks, aircraft and poison gas against the Ethiopian levies, who fought back with suicidal courage and little else.

Conquest of Ethiopia is a complete boxed game in the Panzer Grenadier series based on these battles. You do not need any other game to play any of its 40 scenarios. Design is by Lorenzo Striuli and Ottavio Ricchi, authors of our Fronte Russo supplement.

The Italian invaders rely heavily on colonial troops, long-service professionals recruited in Eritrea, Somalia and Libya. They also have Blackshirts, Carabinieri, elite Alpini mountain troops, tanks and a great deal of air power. And the Italian advantages continue: motor transport, much more artillery and armored cars.

Ethiopia’s defenders include the elite Imperial Guard, and a number of regular battalions that are fairly good troops armed with modern weapons. Beyond that, things get a pretty bad for the Ethiopians pretty quickly. There are three distinct Ethiopian forces at play: the Imperial Regulars, the Imperial Levies (local forces loyal to the throne) and Provincial Levies (local forces loyal to their own leaders, usually a Ras or Duke). None of the levies are very well-armed.

The game includes eight semi-rigid mapboards, with art by Guy Riessen. The maps are fully compatible with all others in the Panzer Grenadier series (and those from Panzer Grenadier (Modern) and Infantry Attacks, too). Plus 517 playing pieces: smooth, scorchless, sootless laser-cut pieces, a joy just to hold in your hands. And 40 scenarios.

This game is sold only direct from Avalanche Press.
