description:
Family Golf  is a board game for up to 4 players. The board is an overview of an 18 hole golf course. Each hole has a different yardage. Along the way, sand traps and water hazards pose problems.

Roll dice to determine yardage of each stroke, and use single die (numbered 1-3) to determine how many putts on green to sink putt.
Total score for each hole then place score on scoresheet (provided). Scoresheet has all 18 holes listed, when all 18 holes completed, tally score, low score wins.

Stroke counters and ball marker provided.  Ball marker is used showing where golfball is located on course, and moved in accordance with yardage determined by each roll of dice (or stroke).
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: In Quest for the Open Tavern players engage in a competitive blind bid character placement game that embraces DIRECT player interaction. This ain’t your dad’s Euro! One player takes the role of the “Wayfarers of Devastation” — a party of Adventurers that recently slew a Great Red Wyrm (or so they claim) and want to celebrate with random acts of drunkenness and senseless vandalism.  But they’ve come to the wrong town! The other player takes the role of Governor Xaggy of Hommlette — he's heard about this band of ne’er-do-wells and he’s ready for them — he will stop at nothing to keep the town safe.  Complicating the issue is the land is under siege from Zombies, Demons, Drunken Wizards, and a well organized Thieves’ Guild, any of which can force the Wayfarers and Governor Xaggy to become uneasy allies.

Tomoe represents the struggle between black and white.
Both players try to encircle with their colour areas as large as they can, without being encircled by the opponents' colour themselves.

The game consists of a board and 32 triple hexagon tiles (4 different tiles, 8 of each).

Players alternately place tiles on the board and eventually also rotate a tile already on the board.
As all tiles contain black and white areas, you can not just build your own area, but always also build something for your opponent. So you have to be very careful what you do, as an enclosed area only counts to your score if it is not enclosed by your opponent itself.

The game ends as soon as the whole board is occupied.
Players count the sectors of their opponents colour in areas they have encircled. The player with the highest score wins.

Hen Haven - Urkunden/ New Haven - Charters consist of 7 tiles which can be achieved during the game.
These tiles show different conditions you have to reach to get additional victory points.

They are scored for being the first to:

- Complete a row of buildings on your Village board
- Complete a column of buildings on your Village board
- Build 6 buildings of the same resource type (colour)

The final charter is the Governor tile. It is worth 2 additional points to any player who has the most charters at the end of the game. In the event of a tie, it is not awarded.

Despite its American inspired name, Chicago has its roots in Sweden and is one of the most popular traditional card games in the country. It has an endless number of variations, so the summary below will deal with the most common rules in use today.

Chicago is played with a standard deck of 52 cards. The object of the game is to score points by having the best poker hand. Each poker hand is assigned a point value (p), as follows:

1p — One Pair: Two cards of the same rank (e.g.: A, 10, 10, 6, 2).
2p — Two Pair: Two pairs of different ranks (e.g.: K, K, J, 4, 4).
3p — Three of a Kind: Three cards of the same rank (e.g.: Q, Q, Q, 9, 6).
4p — Straight: Five cards in rank sequence (e.g.: J, 10, 9, 8, 7).
5p — Flush: Five cards of the same suit (e.g.: A, J, 10, 7, 3; all diamonds).
6p — Full House: Three of a kind in one rank and a pair in a different rank (e.g.: Q, Q, Q, 9, 9). 
7p — Four of a Kind: Four cards of the same rank (e.g.: J, J, J, J, 8).
9p — Straight Flush: A straight with all cards of the same suit (e.g.: J, 10, 9, 8, 7; all diamonds).

Points are also awarded for taking the last trick of each round (5 points), or for scoring a "Chicago" by taking all five tricks of the round (15 or 25 points, must be declared by calling "Chicago" before any trick is played).

The first player to reach a pre-determined number of points (typically 50, 52, or 100) wins the game.

Common scoring variants include awarding an automatic "Chicago" for a holder of a straight flush instead of the normal points awarded for that hand at the end of the round. Others award points equal to the game goal (50, 52, or 100) if the straight flush is also "Royal" (meaning, ranks A, K, Q, J, 10).

Playing a round

Each player (including the dealer) is dealt 5 cards after which 3 phases follow. Before each phase, the players are allowed to discard any unwanted cards face down to the central playing area and are dealt an equal number of replacement cards.

First and Second Phase

Each player may now declare any poker hand which they hold (honor system). Highest hand scores points according to the value of the poker hand combination. The other players do not score. In the case of a tie, highest card used to score the hand wins (highest triple in the event of a full house). If still tied, highest non-scoring card breaks the tie. If still tied, each tied player scores full points.

Variant: If you scored a poker hand, but discard at least one of the scoring cards before the next phase, you must simply announce that you are "not keeping" your hand.

Third Phase

The player to the left of the dealer leads out by playing a card in front of themselves. Each other player must follow suit, if able. The winner of the trick leads out the following trick, and so on.

It is important for all players to keep their own cards separated from other player cards. All played cards should be clearly visible to all players.

At the beginning of the first trick, and only during the first trick, before a player has played their card, they may call "Chicago". Should this happen, all players gather any cards they played back into their hand (as applicable) and the player who called "Chicago" will lead out instead. In order to score a successful "Chicago", all five tricks must be won, no exceptions.

Round Scoring

The winner of the fifth and last trick scores 5 points (variant: score 10 points if you won the trick with a 2). As was done during the first two phases, the best poker hand also scores. If a successful "Chicago" was played, no other points are scored. A failed "Chicago" instead results in the caller receiving a score penalty equal to the points normally awarded (-15 or -25 points). A cumulative negative score is possible.

At the end of scoring, if any player has reached the pre-determined final score, the game ends. Otherwise, or in the event of a tie, play continues. Deal passes to the left. The entire deck is re-shuffled before the initial deal each round.

Kismet is a gambling game.  The object of the game is guess which poker hands will be shown on the next throw of the dice.  The players contribute to form a bank, then make bets at the odds specified on the board.

The dice are made of wood, the one and six are marked in red, there are special markings on the 1, 3, and 6.  The game uses five dice to create poker hands, but it is otherwise unrelated to the well known game Kismet which Spare-Time published in 1963.

Not to be confused with:
Kismet (1963, Spare-Time)
Kismet (1997, Abacus)

Right Connections was one of Springbok's line of "Adult Puzzle-Games." Like some of the others it can be played as a solitaire puzzle or a two-player game.

The pieces are nine plastic disks which each have eight lines radiating from the center, two each in four colors, with the arrangement different on each disk. These are placed on a board with recesses for the disks and lines connecting various spots on the recess edges.

As a puzzle, the object is to connect all the lines of any given color, starting with a random arrangement of disks.

As a game, players take turns starting with a designated disk and line (the lines are designed by black dots on the disks) and turning other disks one by one to make as long a line of the given color as possible. Making a loop scores triple. The player getting to 100 points first wins.

Create routes of cards with photos of places from 56 countries.
Cards are valued from 3000 to 10,000 francs, 7 of each. A route  starts with a transportation card (bus, train, boat or airplane) and 3 cards in sequence or 3 cards with the same value. A route can be continued by the same player or a partner, but it can be stopped by a blocking card (roadblocks, bad weather) unless a defense card can be played immediately.

The winner is the first player or team to earn 150,000 francs.

For 2-6 players, individually or in partnerships.

Pack of promo survivors given to backers of the Zombicide Season 2 Kickstarter campaign.

Contains a total of 12 miniatures, survivor and zombivor versions of each of the following characters: Achille the Serial Killer, Chuck Momma's Boy, Mack the Car Salesman, Smith Dog of War, Mike the Miniature Sculptor, and Gary the Misfit.

- Achille is insane, a demented serial killer, and the zombies didn't change this. He would be a danger to any survivor if he hadn't decided to focus his murderous instinct on zombies. He finds being surrounded by potential victims that are actively hunting him terribly exciting. The human mind has nothing left to teach him. The zombie mind, however, even though it seems simpler, has an interesting potential. Well, you have been warned.

- Against all odds, Chuck has managed to survive the rise of the zombies. Living in his parent's basement, this 30 something man child's lack of real world experience hasn't been that much of a disadvantage, since there isn't much of a world anymore. Armed with a deep knowledge of zombie survival lore gleaned off videogames and the internet, Chuck is an unlikely, chubby ally - and possible food source if things get desperate.

- "Gary, master of none", so people used to say. Gary didn't have a solid education and his demeanor seemed too "cool" to find reliable work and live in the mold of everyday people. He was almost a pariah when the apocalypse struck and shattered the "everyday". Don't be fooled by his outfit: for his casual unconsern, Gary has done well on its own so far. Gary has always been a survivor, and the zombies didn't really change his habbits. If you're open-minded enough to see through Gary's disguise, he could trust you and share his precious tools of the trade.

- Mack's story is like most - an ordinary, happy man untill the zombies rose, forced to dig deep or die. His peacefull previous life hid his true potential - unfortunatly it took burying his wife and two daughters to bring it out. Taking a car from his lot, he began a cross country zombicide, eventually joining the motley crew of survivors. Mack's fighting spirit has made him a precious resource for any team, and his manic grin gets even wider the worse the odds are!

- Working with putty and tiny tools, Mike has been cooped up for years in his home studio sculpting miniatures for boardgames and the like. Obsessed with his craft, he didn't notice when his wife left and took his daughter with her, and he didn't notice the zombie apocalypse either! Well, that is, until he had to stab the mailman in the eye with a sculpting pick, and the wandered outside out of curiousity to find out what was going on. Mike now works with the other survivors, although they're a little disturbed by how much joy he seems to take in the fact that actual real zombies are "finally" running around.

- Smith mustered out of the special forces straight into private "security", working all over the world for the highest bidder. Specializing in wetwork and asking no questions, Smith was in town for a contract when all hell broke loose. Fortunatly for him, and unfortunatly for the zombies, he had his arsenal stashed nearby. Though used to working alone, even Smith knows he doesn't have enough bullets to take on the zombie hordes by himself. Forced to cooperate with the other survivors, he's finding it hard on his limited social skills.

Each character has a unique set of gameplay attributes to give you a unique gaming experience.

A roll and move board game designed in Australia in 1971, this has an interesting mechanic in that there's a separate piece of the board that rotates during play.  It's very reminiscent of an early Talisman, with simpler mechanics.

Athletics is a simulation game of athletics (track & field)

The players pick up a country and start play all the events an athletics meeting or games has (100 meters, 200 meters, High Jump, Triple Jump, Shot Put etc). The players are collecting medals (gold, silver, bronze) and the player with the most medals wins. Also the game have records  for all the events which are close to reality. All the events are played with dice.

A molded plastic base in the bottom half of the box holds wells for cardboard pieces that represent cargos of different shapes (circles, triangles, squares, etc.), different colors, and different numbers.  These wells are covered by a cardboard piece with a spinner and also a train of boxcars of different colors with doors that can each be opened to reveal one well.  The cargos are distributed around the wells at the start of the game.

Players choose a cardboard playing board printed with a train of empty cars pulled by a Chuggington character to collect cargos on.  Then they are then dealt a card showing the pattern of cargos they are to collect on their board. Patterns call for matching by combinations of shape, color, and number of items.

Players take turns spinning the spinner.  Spins allow players to open boxcars to look for a cargo piece that they need for their pattern or rotate the train disk by one or two spaces.  The goal is to be the first player to collect his or her complete pattern.

A memory card game of trying to get your manatees home avoiding getting hit by the boats.
 "The cards are set up in a 5x5 grid. This is the "ocean" where the manatees are swimming. The 4 corners is where the "home" cards go and the very center is a stack of shuffled boat cards that should be face up. All other spaces should each have stacks of two manatee cards on them face down. "
"On your turn, you...

----1. Flip over two manatee cards. If they match, leave them face up. If they do not match, turn them face down again. (OR: if there is a stack of two face-down manatee cards adjacent to an empty space with no manatee cards, you may move the top card from the stack of two into the empty space instead of flipping any cards over.)

----2. If any of YOUR manatees are face up, you may move one of them toward their home location. If you have face up manatee cards that are adjacent to each other, any adjacent manatee can follow the card that moved by moving into the space it just left, and an adjacent manatee can follow that card, and so on. Manatees move up/down and left/right but not diagonal. If a manatee makes it home, that manatee is safe and counts as a point toward winning.

----3. If you moved a manatee you have to move a boat one space too (unless there are no boats left). The boat has to go toward the location matching the manatee on the card, moving up/down and left/right but not diagonal. If the boat lands on the same space as any face-up manatee, the manatee is removed from the game. The boat only moves one space per turn, even if several manatees moved by following the manatee you initially moved. When the boat makes it to the home location, the boat is out of the game.

(Note: You can play competitively or cooperatively. You can move the boats to take your opponents manatees out of the game on purpose or you can work together to have none of the manatees get hit by boats.
 Players keep taking turns until one of the players has all of their manatees out of the game, either from getting hit by a boat or making it safely home. At this point, players should count up all the manatees they got home. Each manatee counts as one point. Whoever has the most points wins."

Mix! is a game of secret goals and tactical color theory, in which players compete to influence which of six paint colors will be applied to canvases in order to meet their own goals. This requires thoughtful play, as the control each player has over which colors are painted is indirect.

At the beginning of the game, each player is assigned a goal with two colors -- one color that will grant them points, and another that will cause them to lose points. A player's positive goal is public, while their negative goal is private. Players must work to influence the painting process so as to maximize their positive goal color, and minimize their negative goal color.

Of course, this is easier said than done. Players can only affect the colors that are painted by controlling the location of a shared set of tokens on a color palette. These tokens are moved between sections of the palette by mixing together color cards from a communal pile that all players must play cards into. Players can also play action cards to affect the location of tokens, and the availability of color cards to mix.

Timing is a critical aspect of Mix!, as players can only take one of three possible actions each turn -- either combining color cards to influence the palette, taking on new bonus objectives, or painting. Painting results in all players gaining and losing points based on the state of the palette, and also allows players to complete their bonus objectives. As a result, players must choose carefully when to work to influence the palette versus when to paint, in order to maximize their own gains while minimizing those of their opponents.

At the end of the game, private goals are revealed, and the player with the most points wins!

The Middle Ages presented many challenges for people in Europe. Day-to-day life was made increasingly difficult when monarchs decided to send all of the able-bodied men off to fight in the Crusades. Could circumstances get even worse? Yes, when a creature of legend arrives at the local castle, eats the Regent and demands tribute. All that’s left to deal with the creature are old men, women, children and cowards. It’s time for the cowards to step up.

As a village elder in this coward-placement game, you are to assign tasks to your village’s cowards. 
Cowards can go to the lists to learn to become warriors, to the forests to become better hunters, to the monasteries to learn about their foe or to get boons, or to the fields to plant and harvest carrots (tribute to the Dragonbunny is paid in carrots…)

Eventually, you will have to send your cowards to face the dreaded Dragonbunny in the castle.
The player whose coward eventually kills the Dragonbunny is the winner.

Game time is 30 minutes per player.

boardgame: Descent: Journeys in the Dark (Second Edition) – Skarn Lieutenant Pack
description:
Skarn's origins lie shrouded in mystery. Some believe that he has always guarded the manor house, even before the death of the High Mage that lived there. Others suggest that he was unintentionally created from residual magic released by the wizard's death. Still others would say that the ravens created a new guardian after the passing of their old master. Whether Skarn is a raging monster hungry for power or a misunderstood soul devoted to a lonely cause, all agree that Skarn's potential stretches far beyond what can be allowed.

Descent: Journeys in the Dark (Second Edition) – Skarn Lieutenant Pack includes a detailed, sculpted plastic figure of Skarn to replace the lieutenant token included in the Manor of Ravens expansion as well as a ten-card Plot deck that lets you spread Skarn's regenerative abilities to the monsters under your command.

This Plot deck also gives you the opportunity to unleash Skarn on Terrinoth by summoning him into battle as your agent in any quest that does not include him as a lieutenant. In battle, heroes will find Skarn a powerful opponent, resilient to damage and able to mend his own wounds in the heat of combat. What's more, Skarn's attacks frequently damage more than one hero, possibly threatening an entire hero party at once if they move in too close.
family: Game: Descent – Journeys in the Dark

description: TouchMoney Game is an educational game designed to teach young children how to count coins.  It is a roll-and-move game where students land on spaces that challenge them to count coins correctly, either from pictures on a card or from coins randomly drawn from a tray of plastic coins on the board.  Players get five points each time they count correctly.  Play ends when a player reaches a certain predetermined number of points or players have circled the board a number of times.  The winner is the player who has earned the most points.

boardgame: Saint Petersburg (Second Edition): Emergency Storehouse Expansion
description:
This expansion adds the Emergency Storehouse tiles to the game.

Components: 2 Emergency Storehouse tiles
(Note: erroneously translated as Emergency Camp in the rules)

At the start of the game, place both Emergency Storehouses next to the game board. An Emergency Storehouse makes it possible for players to keep one more card in hand at a cost, i.e their hand limit is increased by 1 card by paying 1 Ruble upkeep each round.

Also see: Saint Petersburg (Second Edition): The Exclusive Spieleschmiede Expansion

language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
The goal of the game is to move 4 meeples from the center of the labyrinth the finish spaces at the outer rim. The first player to accomplish this wins the game.

The game is played with one die. The meeples may only move in the direction indicated by the arrows on the game board. If a meeple lands on a white dot, it follows the arrow  outwards into the next roundcourse at its next turn. Whenever a meeple lands on an occupied space, it kicks the other meeple back to a starting (a) space of it's color. If a player rolls a 6 he gets an extra roll.

The game was most likely published in the 1930s or early 40s.

This is the first Promo pack for Legends and Lies.  It consists of two additional Anomaly cards, Spontaneous Human Combustion and Message From Beyond, plus one additional Expedition card, Tasmanian Tiger Found! which is for the Skeptic Expansion. Beside the game cards the Promo Pack comes with a Credits/Contact card.

Путешествие в космос (Russian for: Trip to Space). Soviet era board game with simple mechanics and two dice rolled simultaneously. Aimed at familiarizing children with the Solar System (and integer multiplication). The front of the board depicts planets and moons of the Solar System proper and the playing field. On the back of the board are depictions of Soviet space probes and satellites. The goal of the game is to visit the moon of Pluto and return back to Earth.

In this pack of 9 additional adventurers for Dungeon Attack! you will have the chance to take the role of one of these adventurers: the Heroic Paladin, the Shadowy Sorceress, the Deadly Archer, the Crafty Alchemist, the Brutal Brawler, the Primal Druid, the Martial Monk, the Shadow Hunter, and the Valiant Valkyrie.

In addition, four adventurer followers are included along with the optional rules card detailing their use in the game: the Torchbearer Peasant, the Mercenary Sellsword, the Dire Wolf, and the Nurturing Spirit.

Tired of doing everything for chicken feed, the brave chickens leave the coop in search of sweet corn.  Along the way they encounter fury beasts like foxes, skunks, and dogs that must be defeated in order to reach their goal.  Finding useful weapons like bowling pins and ski poles can help.  The support crew lends aid as well.  The fryer can heal them, the gizzard can magically find items, and the Kung Pow Chicken can train the poultry brigade into a stealthy fighting machine.

Spice up your Dungeon Attack! games by using these themed dungeon cards.

Each of the six cards in this pack contains rules that modifies the game. This way every game is slightly different.

There are two ways to use these, randomly pick one for each dungeon level or pick one dungeon card and use it for all the dungeon levels.

Good luck Adventurers!

Skylanders Giants Board Game is a luck based four-in-a-row game. Spin the spinner and place one of your color rings on a Skylander that has the element you've spun. You can even place one of your rings over an opponent's to try to get four in a row faster.

The Skylanders Giants game is designed for two to four players and includes a game board, 24 rings in four different colors, eight giant rings in four different colors, a spinner card and rules for playing.

Need more challenges? Try adding these Poison Dice into your next Dungeon Attack! game.

This set contains x4 Poison Dice and rules card.

In game play each poison hit taken lowers an Adventurer's Combat Rating by 1. So stay away from these poisonous critters or defeat them before they get you.

boardgame: Stalingrad Solitaire: Death Knell of the German 6th Army 1942
description:
Stalingrad Solitaire: Death Knell of the German 6th Army 1942 is a game focusing on the German assault on Stalingrad and focuses on the time period of September 2, 1942 - September 4, 1942. While primarily a solitaire game, the special edition also contains optional two player rules.

The player controls the German forces and must battle the Russian Army to control as many regions of Stalingrad as possible. The game is played by deploying German units on a map of Stalingrad. The AI (or an optional second player) controls the Russian forces through movement, placement, and attack rules.

The game plays out over 15 turns (although shorter scenarios are provided). Each turn, the player will place Russian forces in the Ferry areas on the Volga River (these units are placed upside down and are not revealed until attacked). The German player will then raise troops and move units to instigate combat. The AI rules then dictate how the Russian units will counter attack and conduct movement.

Victory is determined by controlling all the Urban areas of Stalingrad at the end of a turn. The game can end early if the player achieves the victory conditions before the end of turn 15. If the player doesn't control the Urban areas by the end of the game, it is a loss.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

boardgame: Wer reist mit nach Afrika? Ein Unterhaltungs und Belehrungsspiel
description: Players start in Germany. They follow routes through Africa, to numbered circles, and end back on Germany. The board is a map of Europe and Africa, with plants and trees along the bottom. Players roll and move to travel around the board, and then do what the field is saying.

Maritopian Life brings you the first game of its kind; a collectible Trading Card Cannabis Game.

Be a grower. Build your grow room. Create obstacles for your opponents.
Protect your room from other growers. Be the first to score 420 points for the win. For Adults only.

Each deck must have at least 40 cards. Points are accumulated by harvesting plants after the number of turns on a strain card.

In Yoink! players must close their eyes or wear sleep shades and quickly pass tactile cards. Players are not allowed to use their eyes so all players, blind or sighted, are on an equal playing field.

Players must feel the cards to determine when they have cards with all the same or different attributes. In the center of the table there are totems (we use dice) enough for all the players except one. When a player has a set that is all the same(or all different) number of shape, type of shape and background fill they will reach for the totems at the center of the table.

Once the first player has their trio they declare 'Yoink!' and the remaining players scramble to get the remaining totems. Players who don't get a totem are eliminated and play continues.

Countdown: 5 Minutes to Self-Destruction is a real-time cooperative dice-rolling game about escaping a space research station being invaded by alien forms trying to get their hands on human advanced technology. The self-destruction mechanism has been triggered and players only have 5 minutes to kill the aliens blocking their way to the emergency exit hatch.

Players are dealt six face-down alien cards showing the hit pattern to complete in order to kill the alien. The hit patterns consist in 4 or 5 dice in various colors and values. When the game starts, a timer is triggered, all players flip the first card in their deck, and then simultaneously roll dice as fast as they can, in order to complete the hit patterns lying in front of them. Once a pattern is complete, the player flips the next card and starts rolling dice again to complete the new pattern revealed. Dice can (and in some cases must) be passed between players to complete their patterns.

If all players manage to complete all the patterns they were dealt at the beginning of the game, they reach the escape pod safely and leave the space station unharmed. However, if the timer reaches zero before all patterns are completes, the space station explodes with aliens and humans aboard, and everyone lose.

MazeScrolls are a series of maze puzzles games in the form of scrolls. They are a new kind of game genre: the pen and paper maze puzzle dice games for multiple players.

There are 8 different MazeScroll designs and each one uses different graphics and offers different game-play. The eight MazeScrolls are the following:

- Galaxy Far, Faar, Faaar Away
- Perimeter Perplexity
- De Stijl Mile
- Daedalus Treasure
- Lovers Duel
- Daedalus Palace
- Crossing
- Skritches, Skretches and Skrutches

MazeScrolls are a game of meandering journey that can be played by a single player or multiple players. Players can race from one end to the opposite end or they can start at opposite ends and attempt to find each other. Players can also "duel" by first collecting power points so that when they meet-up the one with the most power wins.

Players play the MazeScroll games using a pen, pencil or colored pencils. When the game is done, the finished MazeScroll becomes a piece of artwork that is also a record of their play.

boardgame: Robinson Crusoe: Adventures on the Cursed Island – "King Kong" Scenario
description:
This promo scenario (a one-page printed rule sheet, the back side shows an advertisement for the base game) for "Robinson Crusoe" was released by Pegasus Spiele and contains one new scenario which is quite fitting thematically but also has some humour in it.

This is known as "The Deadly Adventure on King Kong Island" in the Book of Adventures.

From the description:

"You're on a trip with some film makers from Hollywood who want to make a movie about a Giant Ape on a forgotten island. As soon as you arrive, this job seems easier said than done... Can you protect the crew, trap the monster, and make it back to safety alive? Lights, camera, action!"


Dieses Promo-Szenario (ein zweiseitig bedrucktes Regelblatt, die Rückseite enthält Werbung für das Grundspiel) für "Robinson Crusoe" wurde von Pegasus Spiele herausgegeben und enthält ein neues Szenario, welches thematisch durchaus zum Grundspiel paßt, gleichzeitig aber auch eine Prise Humor enthält.

Aus der Beschreibung:

"Ihr seid mit einer Gruppe von Hollywood Filmemachern unterwegs, die einen Film über einen Riesenaffen auf einer verschollenen Insel drehen wollen. Auf der Insel angekommen, erweist sich das als leichter gesagt denn getan... Könnt ihr eure Filmcrew beschützen, das Monster fangen, und die Insel lebendig wieder verlassen? Licht, Kamera, Action!"

family: Game: Robinson Crusoe – Adventures on the Cursed Island

description:
Munchkin Dungeon: Warehouse 23 is a promotional item from Steve Jackson Games that can be used with any Munchkin expansion that contains Dungeons. This item is larger than other Dungeons (6"x4" instead of 4.4"x3.3") and is meant to be kept separate from them. Its text reads:

Any player may choose to put Warehouse 23 into play instead of drawing a Dungeon from the deck. You can never be quite sure what you'll find when you get a box from Warehouse 23! Whenever you are about to draw Treasure as a result of combat, roll the six-sided die and consult this table:


    1 or less - Forget what you were supposed to be drawing, and take one Treasure.
    2 - Draw one Treasure fewer than you otherwise would have.
    3-4 – Draw Treasure normally.
    5 - Draw one Treasure more than you otherwise would have.
    6 or more - Draw twice the number of Treasures you otherwise would have. Score!


When you discard Warehouse 23, put it in the box and forget you ever saw it. Even if several players have Warehouse 23, it can still only show up once per game.

Hundreds of years from now, the greatest sport the galaxy has ever known will explode on your screens as everyone watches their favorite teams of giant Mechs beating each other to scrap. Mechadrome is a "Mech Combat Gladiator Arena Skirmish Game" set in the far future where man and alien races have come together, forming teams that do battle in arenas set in abandoned, war-torn cities and industrial zones.

The rules are simple, fast & fun and you may build your Mechs with a wide variety of devastating weapons, or use our own pre-made teams like the Terran Titans or Team Bushido. The game features a variety of scenarios such as Capture the Flag or No Mech's Land, the objective will always be different, so no game will ever be the same.

Mechadrome Mech models are available in 28mm scale, and made from traditional resin.

MYOB is a Rogue-like, Dungeon Crawler Card Game playable with a standard deck of cards and 5 six-sided Dice.

Single or Multiplayer, MYOB brings the RPG experience in a way that tributes Classic games like Solitaire and Yahtzee.

Players explore a dungeon by drawing cards and placing them on the field to build monsters, and defeating monsters by rolling dice against their Bounty to collect them and their Loot. Loot comprises of Weapon, Armour, and Magic cards. Weapon and Armour cards increase attack and defense, and Magic cards are used to negate combat damage. Defeat both BOSS monsters to win and collect the highest Bounty.

The Advanced Tobruk System (ATS) revisits North Africa with a new collection of scenarios revolving around Rommel's 1941 offensive.  
Six geomorphic desert terrain mapboards, 10 sheets of colour overlays and 3 desert escarpment mapboards provide plenty of new places to wage war.  Four sheets of full-size ATS counters, with new artwork, fill out the British, German and Italian armies of the day.  Also included are 4 sheets of AFV cards, colour enhanced playaid cards and the usual Battlefield Walkaround booklet.

This is not a complete game, as players will need to provide their own ATS rulebook, standard game markers and one or more ten-sided dice.

Bird Whiz is a card-run-making game, published in South Africa in the 1990s (likely 1993 or 1997). It uses colours or you can match them up by type of bird. Once you get a run you put it down, but to keep it in play, you have to answer a question related to the birds in the run. You could skip this part for younger players. With the questions, it makes it an educational game. It is easy to teach and learn. There are also joker cards counting as any card. You score points for the amount of runs you have down. You also score negative points for the cards in your hand once the game ends. The worst card to end up with in your hand is a joker, losing you 50 points. The winner is the one with the most points.

Published about the German City of Kassel.

By playing the players get to know a little bit about the city and its shops.

During each player's turn they roll a six-sided dice to move along a game-board depicting a map of Hannover. At certain spaces players can acquire cards of the shops or they can go sightseeing by following the instructions on the back of the card. Sometimes there is only information written, sometimes you have to pay an amount of money or at other times you may earn some cash.

The game ends when the last player reaches the finish space with his playing piece. The winner is the person who has acquired the most sightseeing and/or shop cards.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements from the Erzgebirge, an area in the south of Saxony.

The Marrying Mr. Darcy: Undead Expansion adds the threat of the walking undead to the heroines' attempts to build their character and marry well. Why not spice up the humdrum of social pleasantries and chasing suitors with the very pressing need to barricade the doors of Pemberley with the piano forte? Yes, the curse of the undead has touched both the women and suitors of Marrying Mr. Darcy in the new Marrying Mr. Darcy Undead Expansion. Stock up on smelling salts to save you from corruption or engage the blighted with cries of, "Tally ho!" as you fight them off with your basket-hilt broadsword. Find the love of your life, but make sure they're not afflicted with the curse of undeath in this quirky expansion to Marrying Mr. Darcy!

description:
Objective:
finish the circuit with less penalties than others
1. build an obstacle course upon a table cloth
2. choose a coloured chip
3. during your turn you must flip a small chip through the circuit
earning penalties for each try (-1), for each damaged obstacle (-2),...

likely published mid. 1950's

boardgame: Kennst Du Sie? ... die Daten der deutschen Geschichte
description:
Objective:
get as many bonus (bright) chips as possible; learn more about history of the German nation and become familiar with the ideology of Nazi Germany
 - acting by turns -
Game master draws a card and ask a player answering the question;
correct? Get a bright chip - Wrong? Get a dark chip. 
Game ends after each player has collected 10 chips. Winner is the one with most bright chips.
Note:
Questions are indoctrinated by Nazi ideology.

The War of 1812 battle collection is similar to the French and Indian War Battle Collection.

War of 1812 contains 12 separate battles with die cut counters terrain chart, rules and scenarios booklet.

This game pack may also be used as a scenario guide for miniature war gaming.

description:
Symetra is a hexagonal-card, glass bead strategy game where players  battle for the opportunity to join Symetra, an ascended being who travels worlds, in battling a high tech evil on her home planet of Orbworld. To prove their worth the challengers must show their skill in wielding Symetra's reality-shaping interfaces that give the user access to the different elements, emotions and energies of the cosmos at a quantum level.

The challengers must actualize their potential energy, represented by their unique colour of glass beads, by fusing, combining and charging cards in the matrix. Once cards are energized in the matrix players can use their unique actions to shape their victory. The player with the most actual energy at the end of the game wins.

Symetra has many different play modes including short and long games, teams, draft, tournament, solitaire and oracle.

Symetra includes 60 unique hexagon-shaped cards, 300 glass beads (6 different coloured sets of 50 beads each) and a detailed rulebook.

description:
The world has been infected with the Madness Virus which has begun turning the population into blood-thirsty zombies. Help find the antidote formula and save the world before it's too late!

Madness is a fast-paced card game for 2 or 4 players ages 9 and up. There are no turns in the game and so players must think and play quickly in order to win. Match your ingredient cards to the ever-changing MADNESS formula before your opponent matches theirs - But be careful, if you select an incorrect formula you lose!

Destruction of Force Z is a tactical level, solitaire simulation of the short air/naval campaign off the coast of Malaya in late 1941 which saw the destruction of two Royal Navy capital ships, HMS Prince of Wales and HMS Repulse by Japanese bombers. The player takes the British side, and the game system plays the Japanese side.

The object of the game is for the British to disrupt Japanese invasions taking place, while not losing his ships. You as the player are presented with a difficult situation. Pearl Harbor has just been attacked; the entire Malayan peninsula is under threat of invasion. There are not many offensive options open to you and your ships, but doing something to take the war to the enemy is preferable to meekly doing nothing at all. Perhaps you can disrupt invasion sites? Intercept enemy warships? Whatever you decide, it will be an uphill battle for the British. In the actual campaign, Force Z sailed out of Singapore into the South China Sea in the early evening of December 8. Admiral Phillips, onboard the Prince of Wales, ordered strict radio silence. If the two British capital ships were able to reach the Gulf of Siam undetected, they might be able to wreak havoc among enemy warships, transports, and invasion sites. If they were found, they risked the wrath of land-based Japanese bombers located in Indo-China. But danger from this eventuality was judged to be minimal, even though the RAF had warned the Admiral that they could not guarantee protection of the ships from the air.

The stage is set, the campaign is about to begin. Can you pull off a British victory, and avoid the destruction of Force Z?

The game includes:

     an illustrated 12-page digest-sized rule booklet that includes all rules and tables necessary for play, 
     a small map and 
     set of 59 counters (which must be printed and separated before play, depending on version), 
     Reference Card, and 
     optional ship counters for a game variant, originally published in Panzerschreck #16 (2014). 


—description from the publisher for 2018 edition

description:
The Crossroads is a classic play of Chinese operas. Ren Tanghui was ordered to protect the banished general Jiao Zan in secret. He stayed overnight at Liu Lihua’s inn. Late at night, they confronted each other in a sudden darkness and dangerous hand-to-hand combat.

The Crossroads is a brief paper-and-pencil game for two players. In a room of 3x3 grids, players take actions concurrently each round and record them on sheets, then make an announcement required to settle their acts.

The basic acts include:

Move: Move to an adjacent grid.

Research: Get the last entry and left directions and round numbers in your position in 8 rounds from the opponent. You have to take a Move act next round.

Attack: Attack the opponent in your position, if he ends round here and doesn’t take an attack action, you win. You have to take a Move act next round.

Pursuit: Move and attack the opponent. You have to stay in the new grid without any actions next round.(3 uses only for each player)

For each Move round a player may also take a Challenge, declare his current position and earn points. In a series of consecutive challenges, it’s 1 point for the first challenge, 2 points for the second, 4 points for the third, 8 points for the fourth and later ones. After 20 rounds without a successful attack, the player has the most points win the game.

It's a game you can wear!  Both tee-shirt and board game, where you try to get your dinosaur to the end before the other players.

(You must provide the die and player markers.)

A Straight Road is a car racing game set in a post-apocalyptic future. Inspired by the countless movies of that genre, you race down a long (it almost seems endless) straight, ever-changing highway, trying to kill off all your competitors, run over the most valuable pedestrians, reach the gas station first or a variety of other goals, depending on the scenario you pick. Sometimes you will be on your own, sometimes you will be part of a gang.

There are many cars, drivers, passengers and equipment available that can be combined freely to build the wheeled killing machine of your dreams. However, the scenario only grants you a certain amount of credit to do so, each car only has a limited load capacity and, of course, the better the gear and the driver, the more expensive.

Game play is simple and fast. Each turn you change your speed, drive and take an action. You attack your opponents with your mounted guns, molotovs, rockets and more, lay mines or leave oil puddles for the guys behind you to slip in, ram other cars and wracks out of your way. Your ability to do stuff changes, depending on how fast you are going and what you are trying to do. However, only in very few cases you will need to calculate anything, most of that is taken care of by the layout of the game material; You just check your current value and roll a six-sided die. While you will want to stay on the road, circumstances often will force you to drive on the shoulder, where going gets rougher and steering more interesting…

Most of the game is free to print and play, you will, however, need small models to represent your cars; Micromachines work well, or any other model of roughly that size. If you deem Thunder Road too simplistic and Car Wars too fiddly, this might be a game for you.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Objective: Gain as much money as possible
- acting by turns -
Players determine a wager; active player throws 2 dice:
On a board - numbered from 3 - 11 (7 in the centre) you have to pay money if the appropiate space/number is empty - collect money - if not. If you throw a 7 you have to pay anyway.
Throwing a 2 you collect all the money without '7', a '12' let you take all. 
note: published before WWII

After scanning many galaxies, many stars, and many planets, you have arrived and found the perfect host to take over humanity. However, you are not the only one. Your probe must compete against others to release virus and take over your host.

Players take on the role of an alien species, controlling a probe that moves around a human's body and releases virus. But, the virus must overcome the body's natural defenses as well as competing for dominance with the other viruses.

Probes arrive in any of the six origin spaces and begin to take over the host's body. As the body's defenses break down, taking over becomes easier until it is taken over completely. The virus that takes over enough body parts, wins the game.

Players must manage a hand of cards, a board position with its virion pieces, and a probe that requires actions to use. Cards synergize well with each other when used in the right configuration and can allow a player to maximize advantages. Also, cards have a different effect when used against the body and when used against other viruses.

Simple indoor ring toss game
- acting by turns -

Players choose a colour and throw plastic rings, hoping to catch one fn the targets and score some points.

The one who scores the most points after a pre-determined time limit runs out, wins.

Note: 
Published before WWII

boardgame: Blood Bowl: Team Manager – The Card Game: Foul Play
description:
Blood Bowl: Team Manager – The Card Game – Foul Play, an expansion for Blood Bowl: Team Manager, introduces the Putrid Players' Guild to the game, with the PPG consisting of three new teams: Nurgle's Rotters, The Lowdown Rats, and The Zharr-Naggrund Ziggurats.

Foul Play also introduces penalties to the game, with players receiving them in various ways, such as by drawing one of the new cheating tokens with the penalty icon or by losing the new Goblin Tribal Leeg tournament.

Finally, Foul Play introduces stadiums, which are constant throughout a season, offer additional payouts to teams, and come with strict rules about who can play in them. A stadium might ban a certain skill, for example, limit the number of players a manager can use, or impose upper or lower limits to Star Power requirements.
